narrative: How you, O Athenians, have been affected by my accusers, I cannot tell; but I know that they almost made me forget who I was—so persuasively did they speak; and yet they have hardly uttered a word of truth. But of the many falsehoods told by them, there was one which quite amazed me;—I mean when they said that you should be upon your guard and not allow yourselves to be deceived by the force of my eloquence. To say this, when they were certain to be detected as soon as I opened my lips and proved myself to be anything but a great speaker, did indeed appear to me most shameless—unless by the force of eloquence they mean the force of truth; for if such is their meaning, I admit that I am eloquent. But in how different a way from theirs! Well, as I was saying, they have scarcely spoken the truth at all; but from me you shall hear the whole truth: not, however, delivered after their manner in a set oration duly ornamented with words and phrases. No, by heaven! but I shall use the words and arguments which occur to me at the moment; for I am confident in the justice of my cause: at my time of life I ought not to be appearing before you, O men of Athens, in the character of a juvenile orator—let no one expect it of me.
refashioned:
How my accusers have affected you, Athenians, I cannot say. I only know they spoke so persuasively they nearly made me forget who I am -- yet hardly a word of it was true. Of their many falsehoods, one amazed me above all: their warning that you should guard against the force of my eloquence. To say this when they were certain to be exposed the moment I opened my mouth and proved myself anything but a great speaker -- that was truly shameless. Unless by the force of eloquence they mean the force of truth, in which case I gladly accept the title. But our eloquence is nothing alike.

As I say, they have spoken almost no truth at all. From me you will hear the whole truth -- though not delivered in their polished style, with carefully ornamented words and phrases. No, by heaven! I will use whatever words and arguments come to me in the moment, because I trust completely in the justice of my cause. At my age, I should not be standing before you, men of Athens, playing the part of some young orator -- do not expect that of me.
elegant: My accusers spoke persuasively but untruthfully. They warned you to guard against the force of my eloquence -- a shameless claim, since I am no polished speaker and they knew I would prove it the moment I opened my mouth. Unless by eloquence they mean the force of truth, in which case I gladly accept the charge. You will hear no rehearsed oration from me, no words polished and arranged for effect, only plain speech as it comes to me, spoken with full confidence in the justice of my cause. At my age, I will not stand before you playing the young orator.
summary: Socrates opens his defense by noting the irony of his accusers warning the jury against his eloquence when he is no skilled orator. He promises to speak plainly and truthfully rather than deliver a polished speech.
distilled: Socrates contrasts his accusers' polished falsehoods with his own plain truth-telling, redefining eloquence as honesty rather than rhetorical skill.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- This opening passage establishes the central rhetorical strategy of the Apology: Socrates presents himself as a plain speaker whose only eloquence is truth, in contrast to the polished but dishonest rhetoric of his accusers. This anti-rhetorical stance is itself a sophisticated rhetorical device, echoing themes from the Gorgias where Socrates distinguishes genuine persuasion from mere flattery. The trial took place in 399 BC before a jury of 501 Athenian citizens. Socrates was approximately 70 years old at the time.

narrative: And I must beg of you to grant me a favour:—If I defend myself in my accustomed manner, and you hear me using the words which I have been in the habit of using in the agora, at the tables of the money-changers, or anywhere else, I would ask you not to be surprised, and not to interrupt me on this account. For I am more than seventy years of age, and appearing now for the first time in a court of law, I am quite a stranger to the language of the place; and therefore I would have you regard me as if I were really a stranger, whom you would excuse if he spoke in his native tongue, and after the fashion of his country:—Am I making an unfair request of you? Never mind the manner, which may or may not be good; but think only of the truth of my words, and give heed to that: let the speaker speak truly and the judge decide justly.
refashioned:
I must ask you a favor. If I defend myself the way I always speak -- using the same language you have heard me use in the marketplace, at the money-changers' tables, and everywhere else -- do not be surprised, and do not interrupt me for it. I am over seventy years old, and this is my first time in a courtroom. I am a complete stranger to legal language. So think of me as a foreigner whom you would excuse for speaking in his own tongue and in the manner of his own country. Is that an unfair request?

Forget about my style -- it may be good or bad. Focus only on whether what I say is true. Let the speaker speak honestly, and let the judge decide justly.
elegant: Grant me this: if I speak in my usual marketplace manner rather than legal language, do not hold it against me. At seventy, this is my first time in court -- I am a stranger here. Judge only whether my words are true, not how they sound. Let the speaker speak honestly and the judge decide justly.
summary: Socrates asks the jury to forgive his informal manner of speaking, comparing himself to a foreigner unfamiliar with courtroom language. He urges them to judge the truth of his words, not his style.
distilled: Socrates asks the jury to judge the truth of his words rather than his informal style, since he is a stranger to courtroom language.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- The agora reference connects to descriptions in the Symposium and other dialogues of Socrates spending his days in public conversation. His claim of courtroom inexperience at age 70 is historically plausible -- Athenian citizens could go their whole lives without appearing in court. The distinction between manner and truth echoes the Gorgias distinction between rhetoric (appearance) and dialectic (reality). The money-changers' tables (trapezai) were informal gathering places in the Athenian agora where business and conversation mixed freely.

narrative: And first, I have to reply to the older charges and to my first accusers, and then I will go on to the later ones. For of old I have had many accusers, who have accused me falsely to you during many years; and I am more afraid of them than of Anytus and his associates, who are dangerous, too, in their own way. But far more dangerous are the others, who began when you were children, and took possession of your minds with their falsehoods, telling of one Socrates, a wise man, who speculated about the heaven above, and searched into the earth beneath, and made the worse appear the better cause. The disseminators of this tale are the accusers whom I dread; for their hearers are apt to fancy that such enquirers do not believe in the existence of the gods. And they are many, and their charges against me are of ancient date, and they were made by them in the days when you were more impressible than you are now—in childhood, or it may have been in youth—and the cause when heard went by default, for there was none to answer. And hardest of all, I do not know and cannot tell the names of my accusers; unless in the chance case of a Comic poet. All who from envy and malice have persuaded you—some of them having first convinced themselves—all this class of men are most difficult to deal with; for I cannot have them up here, and cross-examine them, and therefore I must simply fight with shadows in my own defence, and argue when there is no one who answers. I will ask you then to assume with me, as I was saying, that my opponents are of two kinds; one recent, the other ancient: and I hope that you will see the propriety of my answering the latter first, for these accusations you heard long before the others, and much oftener.
refashioned:
First, I must address the older charges and my earliest accusers before turning to the recent ones. For years, many people have been falsely accusing me to you, and I fear them far more than Anytus and his associates -- dangerous though those men are in their own way. The older accusers are far more dangerous because they got hold of your minds when you were children, filling them with the story of "a certain Socrates, a wise man who speculates about the heavens, investigates what lies beneath the earth, and makes the weaker argument defeat the stronger." These rumor-spreaders are the accusers I truly dread, because people who hear such things naturally assume that anyone who studies nature must not believe in the gods.

These accusers are numerous, their charges go back many years, and they made them when you were most impressionable -- in childhood or youth -- when the case went uncontested because no one was there to answer. Worst of all, I cannot even name them, except perhaps a certain comic playwright. Everyone who has persuaded you out of envy or malice -- some having first convinced themselves -- these people are nearly impossible to deal with. I cannot bring them before you to cross-examine them. I must fight shadows and argue against opponents who do not answer.

So I ask you to recognize, as I said, that my opponents fall into two groups: recent and ancient. And I hope you will agree that I should answer the older accusers first, since you heard their charges long before the others, and far more often.
elegant: I fear the old accusers more than Anytus and his allies. For years they have told you that Socrates is a man who studies the heavens, investigates what lies beneath the earth, and makes the weaker argument defeat the stronger -- and their hearers naturally assume such inquirers deny the gods. These rumors took root when you were impressionable children, went entirely uncontested because no one was there to answer, and I cannot even name the accusers or bring them here to cross-examine them. I must fight shadows and argue when no one answers. Let me address these ancient charges first, since you heard them long before the formal indictment, and far more often.
summary: Socrates distinguishes two classes of accusers: the ancient, unnamed ones who spread rumors about him since the jury's childhood, and the recent formal accusers. He considers the old accusers more dangerous because their slanders went unanswered for decades.
distilled: Socrates identifies his most dangerous enemies as the unnamed, ancient rumor-spreaders who shaped public opinion against him since the jury's childhood, making him fight shadows he cannot cross-examine.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- The "Comic poet" is Aristophanes, whose play Clouds (423 BC) portrayed Socrates as a natural philosopher running a "Thinkery" who taught students to make the weaker argument the stronger. This caricature, presented roughly 24 years before the trial, is what Socrates identifies as the root of popular prejudice against him. The phrase "made the worse appear the better cause" (ton hetto logon kreitto poiein) was associated with the Sophists, particularly Protagoras. Anytus was a wealthy tanner and democratic politician who was the primary force behind the prosecution; he appears in the Meno as hostile to philosophers. The metaphor of "fighting with shadows" (skiamachia) was a term from boxing practice.

narrative: Well, then, I must make my defence, and endeavour to clear away in a short time, a slander which has lasted a long time. May I succeed, if to succeed be for my good and yours, or likely to avail me in my cause! The task is not an easy one; I quite understand the nature of it. And so leaving the event with God, in obedience to the law I will now make my defence.
refashioned: So I must try to undo, in the short time I have, a slander that has built up over many years. May I succeed -- if success would truly be good for both you and me. The task is not easy, and I understand that fully. But leaving the outcome to God, and in obedience to the law, I will now make my defense.
elegant: I must try to undo in minutes a slander built over decades. I understand how difficult this is. Leaving the outcome to God and obeying the law, I begin my defense.
summary: Socrates acknowledges the difficulty of overcoming decades of slander in a short defense, entrusts the outcome to God, and proceeds.
distilled: Socrates acknowledges the near-impossible task of undoing decades of slander in a single speech, then commits himself to the attempt.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- The conditional "if to succeed be for my good and yours" reflects the Socratic principle, developed fully in the Crito and Phaedo, that one should not pursue outcomes without knowing whether they are truly good. Athenian trials allotted limited time for speeches, measured by a water clock (klepsydra). The phrase "leaving the event with God" anticipates the closing words of the Apology: "Which is better God only knows."

narrative: I will begin at the beginning, and ask what is the accusation which has given rise to the slander of me, and in fact has encouraged Meletus to proof this charge against me. Well, what do the slanderers say? They shall be my prosecutors, and I will sum up their words in an affidavit: "Socrates is an evil-doer, and a curious person, who searches into things under the earth and in heaven, and he makes the worse appear the better cause; and he teaches the aforesaid doctrines to others." Such is the nature of the accusation: it is just what you have yourselves seen in the comedy of Aristophanes, who has introduced a man whom he calls Socrates, going about and saying that he walks in air, and talking a deal of nonsense concerning matters of which I do not pretend to know either much or little—not that I mean to speak disparagingly of any one who is a student of natural philosophy. I should be very sorry if Meletus could bring so grave a charge against me. But the simple truth is, O Athenians, that I have nothing to do with physical speculations. Very many of those here present are witnesses to the truth of this, and to them I appeal. Speak then, you who have heard me, and tell your neighbours whether any of you have ever known me hold forth in few words or in many upon such matters...You hear their answer. And from what they say of this part of the charge you will be able to judge of the truth of the rest.
refashioned:
Let me begin at the beginning. What is the accusation that gave rise to this slander and emboldened Meletus to bring his charge? What exactly do the slanderers say? Let them be my prosecutors, and I will summarize their case in a formal charge: "Socrates is an evildoer and a meddler who investigates things beneath the earth and in the heavens, who makes the weaker argument defeat the stronger, and who teaches others to do the same."

That is the accusation -- exactly what you saw in Aristophanes' comedy, where a character called "Socrates" walks about claiming to tread on air and spouting nonsense about matters I know nothing about. I do not mean to disrespect anyone who studies natural philosophy, but the simple truth is, Athenians, that I have nothing to do with such speculations. Many of you here can testify to this. I appeal to you directly: tell your neighbors whether any of you has ever heard me discuss such topics at any length. You hear their answer. And from their testimony on this point, you can judge the truth of the rest.
elegant: What do the old slanderers actually charge? In summary: "Socrates is an evildoer who investigates heaven and earth, makes weak arguments defeat strong ones, and teaches others the same." This is the Socrates of Aristophanes' comedy -- a character who walks on air and talks nonsense about matters I know nothing of. The simple truth is I have nothing to do with natural philosophy, and I do not disrespect those who do. Many of you sitting here can personally confirm that you have never heard me discuss such topics. From their testimony on this point, you may judge the truth of the rest.
summary: Socrates formulates the informal charges against him as a mock indictment, traces them to Aristophanes' caricature, and appeals to the audience as witnesses that he has never engaged in natural philosophy.
distilled: Socrates reframes the popular slander as a formal charge, traces it to Aristophanes' comedic caricature, and appeals to audience members who know he has never studied natural philosophy.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- Aristophanes' Clouds (first performed 423 BC) depicted Socrates suspended in a basket "walking on air" and running a school called the Phrontisterion ("Thinkery") where students learned to make the weaker argument stronger. Socrates distinguishes himself from pre-Socratic natural philosophers like Anaxagoras, Empedocles, and Democritus who studied cosmology and physics. The appeal to the audience as witnesses is a standard Athenian legal technique. Meletus was a young poet who served as the primary named prosecutor.

narrative:
As little foundation is there for the report that I am a teacher, and take money; this accusation has no more truth in it than the other. Although, if a man were really able to instruct mankind, to receive money for giving instruction would, in my opinion, be an honour to him. There is Gorgias of Leontium, and Prodicus of Ceos, and Hippias of Elis, who go the round of the cities, and are able to persuade the young men to leave their own citizens by whom they might be taught for nothing, and come to them whom they not only pay, but are thankful if they may be allowed to pay them. There is at this time a Parian philosopher residing in Athens, of whom I have heard; and I came to hear of him in this way:—I came across a man who has spent a world of money on the Sophists, Callias, the son of Hipponicus, and knowing that he had sons, I asked him: "Callias," I said, "if your two sons were foals or calves, there would be no difficulty in finding some one to put over them; we should hire a trainer of horses, or a farmer probably, who would improve and perfect them in their own proper virtue and excellence; but as they are human beings, whom are you thinking of placing over them? Is there any one who understands human and political virtue? You must have thought about the matter, for you have sons; is there any one?"

"There is," he said.

"Who is he?" said I; "and of what country? and what does he charge?"

"Evenus the Parian," he replied; "he is the man, and his charge is five minae."

Happy is Evenus, I said to myself, if he really has this wisdom, and teaches at such a moderate charge. Had I the same, I should have been very proud and conceited; but the truth is that I have no knowledge of the kind.
refashioned:
Equally baseless is the claim that I am a teacher who charges fees. If someone truly could educate people, taking payment would be perfectly honorable. Consider Gorgias of Leontium, Prodicus of Ceos, and Hippias of Elis -- they travel from city to city, and young men willingly leave their own fellow citizens, who would teach them for free, to study with these men and pay handsomely for the privilege.

There is also a philosopher from Paros currently living in Athens. I heard about him this way: I ran into Callias, son of Hipponicus -- a man who has spent a fortune on Sophists. Knowing he had sons, I asked him: "Callias, if your two sons were foals or calves, we could easily find someone to train them -- a horse-trainer or farmer who would develop their natural excellence. But since they are human beings, who are you planning to put in charge of them? Is there anyone who understands human and civic virtue? Surely you have thought about this, since you have sons."

"There is," he said.

"Who is he, where is he from, and what does he charge?" I asked.

"Evenus the Parian," he replied. "He is the man, and his fee is five minas."

How fortunate Evenus is, I thought, if he truly possesses this wisdom and teaches it so reasonably. If I had such knowledge, I would be enormously proud. But the truth is, I have no such expertise.
elegant: The claim that I teach for money is equally false. If someone could truly educate people, charging fees would be an honor -- as the Sophists Gorgias, Prodicus, and Hippias do, traveling from city to city while young men gladly pay them. I once asked Callias, who has spent a fortune on Sophists: if your two sons were horses or calves, you would find a trainer to develop their proper excellence. Since they are human beings, who will teach them human and civic virtue? He named Evenus the Parian, who charges five minas. How fortunate Evenus is, if he truly possesses such wisdom and teaches it at so reasonable a price. I certainly possess no such knowledge.
summary: Socrates denies being a paid teacher, distinguishes himself from the professional Sophists, and recounts his conversation with Callias about who can teach human virtue -- concluding that he himself possesses no such expertise.
distilled: Socrates denies being a paid teacher and distinguishes himself from the Sophists by insisting he possesses no teachable expertise in human virtue.
notes: Speaker: Socrates, with dialogue involving Callias. -- Gorgias, Prodicus, and Hippias were the most famous Sophists of the 5th century; each appears in Platonic dialogues named after them (Gorgias, Protagoras where Prodicus appears, and Hippias Major/Minor). Callias, son of Hipponicus, was the wealthiest man in Athens and a famous patron of Sophists; he hosts the gathering in Plato's Protagoras. Evenus of Paros was a poet and teacher; Socrates mentions him again in the Phaedo (61c) just before his death. Five minas (500 drachmas) was a substantial but not exorbitant fee -- roughly a year's wages for a skilled laborer. The animal-trainer analogy anticipates the horse-trainer argument Socrates will use against Meletus in 1.17.

narrative: I dare say, Athenians, that some one among you will reply, "Yes, Socrates, but what is the origin of these accusations which are brought against you; there must have been something strange which you have been doing? All these rumours and this talk about you would never have arisen if you had been like other men: tell us, then, what is the cause of them, for we should be sorry to judge hastily of you." Now I regard this as a fair challenge, and I will endeavour to explain to you the reason why I am called wise and have such an evil fame. Please to attend then. And although some of you may think that I am joking, I declare that I will tell you the entire truth. Men of Athens, this reputation of mine has come of a certain sort of wisdom which I possess. If you ask me what kind of wisdom, I reply, wisdom such as may perhaps be attained by man, for to that extent I am inclined to believe that I am wise; whereas the persons of whom I was speaking have a superhuman wisdom which I may fail to describe, because I have it not myself; and he who says that I have, speaks falsely, and is taking away my character. And here, O men of Athens, I must beg you not to interrupt me, even if I seem to say something extravagant. For the word which I will speak is not mine. I will refer you to a witness who is worthy of credit; that witness shall be the God of Delphi—he will tell you about my wisdom, if I have any, and of what sort it is. You must have known Chaerephon; he was early a friend of mine, and also a friend of yours, for he shared in the recent exile of the people, and returned with you. Well, Chaerephon, as you know, was very impetuous in all his doings, and he went to Delphi and boldly asked the oracle to tell him whether—as I was saying, I must beg you not to interrupt—he asked the oracle to tell him whether anyone was wiser than I was, and the Pythian prophetess answered, that there was no man wiser. Chaerephon is dead himself; but his brother, who is in court, will confirm the truth of what I am saying.
refashioned:
I expect some of you are thinking: "Yes, Socrates, but where did these accusations come from? You must have been doing something unusual. These rumors would never have started if you were like everyone else. Tell us the real cause, because we do not want to judge you unfairly."

That is a fair challenge, and I will try to explain why I am called wise and have such a bad reputation. Pay close attention. Some of you may think I am joking, but I swear I will tell you the complete truth.

Athenians, my reputation comes from a certain kind of wisdom I possess. What kind? The kind that may be within human reach -- to that extent, I believe I am wise. The Sophists I mentioned claim a wisdom beyond human capacity, which I cannot describe because I do not have it. Anyone who says I do is lying and misrepresenting me.

Please do not interrupt me, even if what I say sounds outrageous. The testimony I am about to offer is not my own -- I will call a witness of the highest credibility: the god of Delphi himself. He will tell you about my wisdom, if I have any, and what kind it is.

You knew Chaerephon. He was an old friend of mine, and a friend of the people too -- he went into exile with the democrats and returned with you. Well, Chaerephon was impetuous in everything he did, and one day he went to Delphi and had the audacity to ask the oracle whether anyone was wiser than I was. And the Pythian priestess answered that no one was wiser. Chaerephon himself has died, but his brother, who is here in court, will confirm this.
elegant: You may ask: where did these accusations come from? You must have been doing something unusual. Fair enough -- I will explain, and though it sounds extravagant, every word is true. My reputation comes from a certain kind of wisdom -- the human kind, not the superhuman wisdom the Sophists claim to possess. The word I speak is not my own; my witness is the god of Delphi himself. You knew my friend Chaerephon -- impetuous in everything he did, a friend of the people who shared in your recent exile and returned with you. He went to Delphi and boldly asked the oracle whether anyone was wiser than Socrates. The Pythian priestess answered that no one was wiser. Chaerephon is dead, but his brother, who is here in court, will confirm the truth of this.
summary: Socrates explains the origin of his reputation by introducing the story of the Delphic Oracle. His friend Chaerephon asked if anyone was wiser than Socrates, and the oracle answered no. He distinguishes his human wisdom from the supposed superhuman wisdom of the Sophists.
distilled: Socrates traces his reputation to the Oracle at Delphi, which declared that no one was wiser than he -- a claim he offers not as boast but as puzzle requiring explanation.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- Chaerephon was a lifelong friend of Socrates, known for his passionate temperament; Aristophanes mocked him in several plays. The "recent exile" refers to the period of the Thirty Tyrants (404-403 BC), when democratic Athenians fled and later returned to restore democracy. The Oracle at Delphi (the Pythia) was the most authoritative religious institution in ancient Greece; Apollo spoke through the priestess. The oracle's pronouncement is the pivotal moment of the Apology -- it sets the entire narrative of Socrates' philosophical mission in motion. Socrates' distinction between human and superhuman wisdom anticipates his conclusion that true wisdom consists in knowing one's ignorance.

narrative: Why do I mention this? Because I am going to explain to you why I have such an evil name. When I heard the answer, I said to myself, "What can the god mean? and what is the interpretation of his riddle? for I know that I have no wisdom, small or great. What then can he mean when he says that I am the wisest of men? And yet he is a god, and cannot lie; that would be against his nature." After long consideration, I thought of a method of trying the question. I reflected that if I could only find a man wiser than myself, then I might go to the god with a refutation in my hand. I should say to him, "Here is a man who is wiser than I am; but you said that I was the wisest." Accordingly I went to one who had the reputation of wisdom, and observed him—his name I need not mention; he was a politician whom I selected for examination—and the result was as follows: When I began to talk with him, I could not help thinking that he was not really wise, although he was thought wise by many, and still wiser by himself; and thereupon I tried to explain to him that he thought himself wise, but was not really wise; and the consequence was that he hated me, and his enmity was shared by several who were present and heard me. So I left him, saying to myself, as I went away: "Well, although I do not suppose that either of us knows anything really beautiful and good, I am better off than he is,—for he knows nothing, and thinks that he knows; I neither know nor think that I know. In this latter particular, then, I seem to have slightly the advantage of him." Then I went to another who had still higher pretensions to wisdom, and my conclusion was exactly the same. Whereupon I made another enemy of him, and of many others besides him.
refashioned:
Why do I mention the oracle? Because it explains why I have such a bad reputation. When I heard the answer, I thought: what can the god possibly mean? What is the solution to this riddle? I know I have no wisdom, great or small. What does he mean by calling me the wisest of men? Yet he is a god and cannot lie -- that would be against his nature.

After thinking about it for a long time, I devised a way to test the oracle. If I could find a man wiser than myself, I could go back to the god with a refutation: "Here is someone wiser than I am, but you said I was the wisest."

So I went to a man with a reputation for wisdom -- a politician, whose name I need not mention -- and examined him. The result: when I talked with him, I realized he was not actually wise, though many people thought so, and he himself was most convinced of all. When I tried to show him that he only thought he was wise without being so, he came to hate me, and so did others who were present.

As I walked away, I reflected: neither of us knows anything truly worthwhile, but I am better off than he is. He knows nothing yet thinks he knows. I neither know nor think I know. In this one respect, I have a slight advantage.

Then I went to another man with even greater pretensions to wisdom, and reached the same conclusion. And so I made yet another enemy -- and many more besides.
elegant: The oracle's answer puzzled me: I knew I had no wisdom, small or great. But a god cannot lie -- that would be against his nature. So after long thought I devised a test: find someone wiser and refute the oracle. I examined a respected politician and found he was not truly wise, though many thought so and he himself was most convinced of all. When I tried to show him this, he came to hate me, along with others who were listening. Walking away, I reflected: neither of us knows anything truly worthwhile, but I am slightly better off. He knows nothing and thinks he knows; I neither know nor think I know. I went to another man with still higher pretensions to wisdom, reached the same conclusion, and made yet more enemies.
summary: Puzzled by the oracle, Socrates devises a method to test it: find someone wiser. He examines a politician and discovers the man is not truly wise but thinks he is. Socrates concludes his only advantage is knowing his own ignorance.
distilled: Socrates tests the oracle by examining a supposedly wise politician, discovers the man's wisdom is illusory, and concludes that knowing one's own ignorance is itself a form of wisdom.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- This passage introduces the Socratic method in its original context: elenchus (cross-examination) as a divine mission. The unnamed politician may be a composite figure or a specific individual Socrates chose not to name. The conclusion -- "I neither know nor think that I know" -- is the foundation of Socratic epistemology, echoed throughout the dialogues, particularly in the Meno (where Socrates demonstrates that recognizing ignorance is the beginning of learning) and the Republic (where the philosopher-king must first recognize the limits of human knowledge). The principle that gods cannot lie reflects traditional Greek theology and is developed further in Republic Book II.

narrative: Then I went to one man after another, being not unconscious of the enmity which I provoked, and I lamented and feared this: but necessity was laid upon me,—the word of God, I thought, ought to be considered first. And I said to myself, "Go I must to all who appear to know, and find out the meaning of the oracle." And I swear to you, Athenians, by the dog I swear!—for I must tell you the truth—the result of my mission was just this: I found that the men most in repute were all but the most foolish; and that others less esteemed were really wiser and better. I will tell you the tale of my wanderings and of the "Herculean" labours, as I may call them, which I endured only to find at last the oracle irrefutable. After the politicians, I went to the poets; tragic, dithyrambic, and all sorts. And there, I said to myself, you will be instantly detected; now you will find out that you are more ignorant than they are. Accordingly, I took them some of the most elaborate passages in their own writings, and asked what was the meaning of them—thinking that they would teach me something. Will you believe me? I am almost ashamed to confess the truth, but I must say that there is hardly a person present who would not have talked better about their poetry than they did themselves. Then I knew that not by wisdom do poets write poetry, but by a sort of genius and inspiration; they are like diviners or soothsayers who also say many fine things, but do not understand the meaning of them. The poets appeared to me to be much in the same case; and I further observed that upon the strength of their poetry they believed themselves to be the wisest of men in other things in which they were not wise. So I departed, conceiving myself to be superior to them for the same reason that I was superior to the politicians.
refashioned:
I went from one person to the next, fully aware of the hostility I was creating. I lamented it and feared it, but I felt compelled -- the word of God, I believed, must come first. I told myself: I must go to everyone who claims to know something and discover what the oracle means.

And I swear to you, Athenians -- by the dog, I swear! -- the result of my mission was this: the men with the greatest reputations turned out to be the most foolish, while those held in lower regard were actually wiser and better.

Let me tell you about my wanderings -- my Herculean labors, you might call them -- which I endured only to find the oracle could not be refuted.

After the politicians, I went to the poets: tragedians, dithyrambic poets, all kinds. Here, I thought, I would surely be exposed. Now I would discover that I was more ignorant than they were. I took some of their most elaborate passages and asked what they meant, expecting them to teach me something. Will you believe me? I am almost ashamed to say it, but almost anyone in this audience could have discussed their poetry better than the poets themselves. I realized then that poets do not write from wisdom but from a kind of natural genius and inspiration -- like prophets and soothsayers who say beautiful things without understanding them. The poets were in the same condition, and worse: their poetic gift made them believe they were the wisest of men in all other matters too, when they were not. So I left them, judging myself superior for the same reason I was superior to the politicians.
elegant: I continued my investigation despite the enmity it caused, because the word of God must come first. The result of my Herculean labors: the most reputed men were the most foolish; the least esteemed were wiser. After politicians, I examined poets. I asked them to explain their finest passages and found that almost anyone present could discuss their work better than they could. Poets write not from wisdom but from inspiration, like prophets who say fine things without understanding them. And their gift made them think they were wise in everything else too.
summary: Socrates continues his investigation, examining poets after politicians. He discovers they cannot explain their own works and write from inspiration rather than understanding, yet their talent makes them believe they are wise in all things.
distilled: Socrates finds that poets create through inspiration rather than understanding, and their creative gift delusionally convinces them of universal wisdom.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- "By the dog" (ne ton kuna) was Socrates' characteristic mild oath, sometimes rendered "by the dog of Egypt" (referring to Anubis), used to avoid swearing by the Olympian gods. The Herculean labors metaphor frames his philosophical mission as an epic ordeal. Dithyrambic poetry was choral lyric performed at festivals of Dionysus. The theory that poets write by divine inspiration rather than craft appears also in the Ion, where Socrates argues that poets are possessed by the Muse and serve as channels for divine utterance without personal understanding. This view contrasts with Aristotle's later treatment of poetry as a rational craft in the Poetics.

narrative: At last I went to the artisans. I was conscious that I knew nothing at all, as I may say, and I was sure that they knew many fine things; and here I was not mistaken, for they did know many things of which I was ignorant, and in this they certainly were wiser than I was. But I observed that even the good artisans fell into the same error as the poets;—because they were good workmen they thought that they also knew all sorts of high matters, and this defect in them overshadowed their wisdom; and therefore I asked myself on behalf of the oracle, whether I would like to be as I was, neither having their knowledge nor their ignorance, or like them in both; and I made answer to myself and to the oracle that I was better off as I was.
refashioned:
Finally, I went to the artisans. I knew I was ignorant of practically everything, and I was sure they would know many valuable things. I was right -- they did possess knowledge I lacked, and in that respect they were genuinely wiser than I was.

But I noticed that even the best craftsmen fell into the same error as the poets: because they excelled at their craft, they assumed they also understood all sorts of grand and important matters. This flaw overshadowed their genuine expertise.

So I asked myself, on the oracle's behalf: would I rather be as I am -- without their knowledge but also without their false presumption -- or like them, possessing both? I answered myself, and the oracle, that I was better off as I was.
elegant: The artisans genuinely knew things I did not -- they were wiser in their craft. But like the poets, their skill made them presume expertise in everything else. I asked myself: would I rather have their knowledge along with their false presumption, or remain as I am, without either? I chose to remain as I was.
summary: Socrates examines the artisans and finds they possess genuine craft knowledge but overextend it, presuming expertise in all matters. He concludes he is better off with his honest ignorance than with their knowledge paired with false presumption.
distilled: The artisans possess genuine craft knowledge but fatally overextend it into domains beyond their expertise, making honest ignorance preferable to knowledgeable presumption.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- The artisans (demiourgoi) represent the third class Socrates examines, after politicians and poets. Unlike the others, he grants them genuine knowledge in their domain -- a concession that anticipates the Republic's tripartite division of the ideal state into rulers, auxiliaries, and producers. The error of the artisans -- overextending domain expertise into universal wisdom -- is a cognitive bias that remains relevant. The passage establishes a hierarchy: politicians had no real knowledge; poets had inspired creation without understanding; artisans had genuine craft knowledge but overreached. Only Socrates possessed what might be called epistemic humility.

narrative: This inquisition has led to my having many enemies of the worst and most dangerous kind, and has given occasion also to many calumnies. And I am called wise, for my hearers always imagine that I myself possess the wisdom which I find wanting in others: but the truth is, O men of Athens, that God only is wise; and by his answer he intends to show that the wisdom of men is worth little or nothing; he is not speaking of Socrates, he is only using my name by way of illustration, as if he said, "He, O men, is the wisest, who, like Socrates, knows that his wisdom is in truth worth nothing." And so I go about the world, obedient to the god, and search and make enquiry into the wisdom of any one, whether citizen or stranger, who appears to be wise; and if he is not wise, then in vindication of the oracle I show him that he is not wise; and my occupation quite absorbs me, and I have no time to give either to any public matter of interest or to any concern of my own, but I am in utter poverty by reason of my devotion to the god.
refashioned:
This investigation has earned me many dangerous enemies and much slander. People call me wise because they assume I must possess the wisdom I find lacking in others. But the truth, Athenians, is that only God is truly wise. The oracle's answer was meant to show that human wisdom is worth little or nothing. He was not really talking about Socrates -- he was using my name as an example, as if to say: "The wisest among you is the one who, like Socrates, recognizes that his wisdom is truly worth nothing."

And so I go about, obedient to the god, examining anyone -- citizen or foreigner -- who seems to be wise. When I find that someone is not wise, I demonstrate it in service of the oracle. This work consumes me entirely. I have no time for public affairs or my own private interests, and I live in complete poverty because of my devotion to the god.
elegant: My investigation has made me many enemies. People mistakenly think I possess the wisdom I find lacking in others. But only God is wise. The oracle used my name as an illustration: the wisest person is the one who knows his wisdom is worth nothing. So I go about examining everyone, obedient to the god. This mission consumes me entirely, leaving me in poverty.
summary: Socrates interprets the oracle's meaning: only God is truly wise, and human wisdom is worth little. The oracle used Socrates as an example because he alone recognizes his own ignorance. His obedience to this divine mission has consumed his life and left him in poverty.
distilled: Socrates concludes that the oracle's true message is that only God is wise, and the wisest human is the one who recognizes his own wisdom is worth nothing -- a mission that has consumed his life.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- This passage provides Socrates' definitive interpretation of the oracle and his life's mission. The claim that "God only is wise" places human wisdom in radical perspective and establishes the theological foundation of Socratic philosophy. The poverty caused by his mission is confirmed by multiple sources; in the Symposium, Alcibiades describes Socrates going barefoot and wearing a single worn cloak. The phrase about having no time for public affairs anticipates his later discussion (1.36) of why the divine sign prevented him from entering politics. This passage also establishes Socrates' philosophical activity as religious obedience -- a point crucial to his defense against the charge of impiety.

narrative: There is another thing:—young men of the richer classes, who have not much to do, come about me of their own accord; they like to hear the pretenders examined, and they often imitate me, and proceed to examine others; there are plenty of persons, as they quickly discover, who think that they know something, but really know little or nothing; and then those who are examined by them instead of being angry with themselves are angry with me: "This confounded Socrates," they say; "this villainous misleader of youth!"—and then if somebody asks them, "Why, what evil does he practise or teach?" they do not know, and cannot tell; but in order that they may not appear to be at a loss, they repeat the ready-made charges which are used against all philosophers about teaching things up in the clouds and under the earth, and having no gods, and making the worse appear the better cause; for they do not like to confess that their pretence of knowledge has been detected—which is the truth; and as they are numerous and ambitious and energetic, and are drawn up in battle array and have persuasive tongues, they have filled your ears with their loud and inveterate calumnies. And this is the reason why my three accusers, Meletus and Anytus and Lycon, have set upon me; Meletus, who has a quarrel with me on behalf of the poets; Anytus, on behalf of the craftsmen and politicians; Lycon, on behalf of the rhetoricians: and as I said at the beginning, I cannot expect to get rid of such a mass of calumny all in a moment. And this, O men of Athens, is the truth and the whole truth; I have concealed nothing, I have dissembled nothing. And yet, I know that my plainness of speech makes them hate me, and what is their hatred but a proof that I am speaking the truth?—Hence has arisen the prejudice against me; and this is the reason of it, as you will find out either in this or in any future enquiry.
refashioned:
There is something else. Young men from wealthy families, with time on their hands, follow me around of their own accord. They enjoy watching me examine pretenders, and they often imitate me, going out and examining others themselves. They quickly discover no shortage of people who think they know something but really know little or nothing. Those who get exposed become angry -- not at themselves, but at me. "This wretched Socrates," they say, "this corrupt misleader of the young!" And when someone asks them, "What evil does he actually do or teach?" they have no answer. But rather than admit their pretense of knowledge has been exposed, they reach for the stock accusations used against all philosophers: teaching about things in the sky and underground, denying the gods, making weak arguments defeat strong ones.

These people are numerous, ambitious, energetic, organized, and persuasive. They have filled your ears with their persistent slander. This is why my three accusers have come after me: Meletus on behalf of the poets, Anytus on behalf of the craftsmen and politicians, Lycon on behalf of the orators. As I said at the beginning, I cannot expect to clear away such an accumulation of slander in a short time.

This, Athenians, is the truth and the whole truth. I have hidden nothing and dissembled nothing. And yet I know that my plain speaking makes them hate me all the more -- but what is their hatred if not proof that I am telling the truth? This is the origin of the prejudice against me, as you will discover in this or any future inquiry.
elegant: Wealthy young men follow me of their own accord, enjoy watching me examine pretenders, and then imitate me by examining others. They quickly find no shortage of people who think they know something but do not. Those exposed become angry at me rather than at themselves, calling me a villainous misleader of youth. When asked what evil I actually do or teach, they have no answer, so they repeat the stock charges used against all philosophers: teaching about things in the sky and underground, denying the gods, making weak arguments strong. These people are numerous, ambitious, organized, and persuasive. They have filled your ears with persistent slander. This is why my three accusers have set upon me: Meletus on behalf of the poets, Anytus for the craftsmen and politicians, Lycon for the orators. Their hatred is itself proof that I speak the truth.
summary: Socrates explains that young imitators expose people's ignorance, creating resentment directed at him. The exposed resort to generic anti-philosopher accusations. He identifies his three accusers as representing the groups he has offended: poets, craftsmen/politicians, and orators.
distilled: Those exposed by Socrates' young imitators blame him rather than face their own ignorance, generating the slander that his three accusers -- representing poets, politicians, and orators -- have weaponized against him.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- This passage explains the social mechanism behind the accusations. The three accusers each represent a class Socrates examined: Meletus was a minor poet; Anytus was a prominent tanner and democratic politician who appears in the Meno (90a-95a) where he threatens Socrates for criticizing Athenian statesmen; Lycon was an orator about whom little else is known. The young followers who imitate Socrates include figures like Plato, Xenophon, Alcibiades, and Critias -- though the latter two became politically controversial, which contributed to public suspicion of Socrates. The "ready-made charges" (stock accusations against philosophers) echo the comic tradition represented by Aristophanes' Clouds.

narrative: I have said enough in my defence against the first class of my accusers; I turn to the second class. They are headed by Meletus, that good man and true lover of his country, as he calls himself. Against these, too, I must try to make a defence:—Let their affidavit be read: it contains something of this kind: it says that "Socrates is a doer of evil, who corrupts the youth; and who does not believe in the gods of the state, but has other new divinities of his own." Such is the charge; and now let us examine the particular counts. He says that I am a doer of evil, and corrupt the youth; but I say, O men of Athens, that Meletus is a doer of evil, in that he pretends to be in earnest when he is only in jest, and is so eager to bring men to trial from a pretended zeal and interest about matters in which he really never had the smallest interest. And the truth of this I will endeavour to prove to you.
refashioned:
I have said enough in answer to the first class of accusers. Now I turn to the second class, led by Meletus -- that good man and true patriot, as he styles himself. Against these too, I must mount a defense.

Let the formal indictment be read. It says something like this: "Socrates is an evildoer who corrupts the youth and does not believe in the gods of the state, but introduces other new spiritual beings in their place."

That is the charge. Now let us examine each count. He says I do evil and corrupt the youth. But I say, Athenians, that Meletus is the one doing evil -- by pretending to be serious when he is merely playing games, and by dragging men into court with a show of zeal and concern for matters he has never actually cared about. I will endeavor to prove this to you.
elegant: I turn now to the formal accusers, led by Meletus, who calls himself a patriot. The indictment charges that Socrates corrupts the youth and substitutes new spiritual beings for the state's gods. But I say Meletus is the real evildoer -- pretending seriousness about matters he has never genuinely cared about. I will prove this.
summary: Socrates transitions from defending against informal slander to addressing the formal indictment brought by Meletus, which charges him with corrupting youth and religious innovation. He countercharges that Meletus is the true evildoer, feigning concern he does not actually possess.
distilled: Socrates pivots to the formal indictment -- corruption of youth and religious innovation -- and countercharges that Meletus himself is the real evildoer, pretending concern about matters he has never cared about.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- This marks the transition from the defense against popular prejudice (the "first accusers") to the defense against the formal legal charges. The actual indictment, preserved by Diogenes Laertius and Xenophon, read approximately: "Socrates is guilty of not acknowledging the gods the city acknowledges, and of introducing other new spiritual beings (daimonia kaina). He is also guilty of corrupting the young." The charge of introducing "new divinities" likely referred to Socrates' daimonion -- his inner divine sign. Meletus was a young, relatively unknown poet; Socrates' sarcastic description of him as a "good man and true lover of his country" is ironic. The Euthyphro is set just before the trial and explores the nature of piety in connection with these charges.

narrative:
"Come hither, Meletus, and let me ask a question of you. You think a great deal about the improvement of youth?"

"Yes, I do."
refashioned:
"Come here, Meletus, and let me ask you a question. You care a great deal about the improvement of the young?"

"Yes, I do."
elegant: "Come, Meletus. You care greatly about improving the young?" "Yes."
summary: Socrates begins his cross-examination of Meletus by establishing that Meletus claims to care about the improvement of youth.
distilled: Socrates opens his cross-examination by getting Meletus to affirm his concern for the youth -- the premise Socrates will systematically dismantle.
notes: Speaker: Socrates and Meletus. -- This begins the elenctic cross-examination of Meletus, a dramatic centerpiece of the Apology. In Athenian trials, the defendant had the right to question his accuser directly. Socrates employs the same dialectical method he uses in the philosophical dialogues -- beginning with the interlocutor's own claims and drawing out contradictions. The question about caring for youth is strategic: by getting Meletus to affirm strong concern for youth improvement, Socrates sets up the horse-trainer analogy that will expose Meletus' ignorance of the very subject he claims to champion.

narrative: "Tell the judges, then, who is their improver; for you must know, as you have taken the pains to discover their corrupter, and are citing and accusing me before them. Speak, then, and tell the judges who their improver is."—Observe, Meletus, that you are silent, and have nothing to say. But is not this rather disgraceful, and a very considerable proof of what I was saying, that you have no interest in the matter? "Speak up, friend, and tell us who their improver is."
refashioned:
"Then tell the judges who improves the youth. You must know, since you took the trouble to identify their corrupter and bring me before this court. Speak -- tell the judges who their improver is."

Observe, Meletus, that you are silent. You have nothing to say. Is this not disgraceful? Is it not considerable proof of exactly what I was arguing -- that you have no real interest in this matter?

"Speak up, friend, and tell us who improves the young."
elegant: "You identified their corrupter -- so tell the judges who improves them." Meletus is silent. His silence proves my point: he has never actually cared about this matter. "Speak up and tell us."
summary: Socrates demands that Meletus identify who improves the youth, since he claims to have identified their corrupter. Meletus' silence is taken as proof of his indifference to the subject.
distilled: Socrates exposes Meletus' silence on who improves youth as proof that a man who claims to know the corrupter has never thought about the improver.
notes: Speaker: Socrates, addressing Meletus. -- The argumentative structure is characteristically Socratic: if you know what is harmful, you should know what is beneficial. This mirrors the craft analogy -- a doctor must know both disease and health. Meletus' hesitation suggests he was unprepared for cross-examination, which supports Socrates' claim that the prosecution was not genuinely motivated by concern for the youth. The technique of exposing an opponent's ignorance through their own silence appears throughout the dialogues, notably in the Gorgias where Callicles eventually falls silent.

narrative:
"The laws."

But that, my good sir, is not my meaning. I want to know who the person is, who, in the first place, knows the laws.

"The judges, Socrates, who are present in court."

"What, do you mean to say, Meletus, that they are able to instruct and improve youth?"

"Certainly they are."

"What, all of them, or some only and not others?"

"All of them."

"By the goddess Here, that is good news! There are plenty of improvers, then. And what do you say of the audience,—do they improve them?"

"Yes, they do."

"And the senators?"

"Yes, the senators improve them."

"But perhaps the members of the assembly corrupt them?—or do they too improve them?"

"They improve them."

"Then every Athenian improves and elevates them; all with the exception of myself; and I alone am their corrupter? Is that what you affirm?"

"That is what I stoutly affirm."
refashioned:
"The laws."

"That is not what I am asking, my good sir. I want to know the person who knows the laws -- who is the improver?"

"The judges, Socrates -- those present in this court."

"You mean to say that the judges are able to instruct and improve the young?"

"Certainly."

"All of them, or only some?"

"All of them."

"By Hera, that is wonderful news! No shortage of improvers, then. And the audience here -- do they improve the young too?"

"Yes, they do."

"And the senators?"

"Yes, the senators improve them."

"And what about the members of the Assembly? Do they corrupt them, or do they also improve them?"

"They improve them."

"So every Athenian improves and elevates the young -- every single one except me. I alone am their corrupter. Is that your claim?"

"That is exactly what I claim."
elegant: Pressed to name who improves youth, Meletus says the laws, then the judges, then the audience, then the senators, then the Assembly members. Socrates draws him into claiming that every single Athenian improves the young -- all except Socrates, who alone corrupts them.
summary: Through rapid questioning, Socrates leads Meletus to claim that every Athenian -- judges, audience, senators, assemblymen -- improves the youth, with Socrates as the sole exception and sole corrupter.
distilled: Socrates traps Meletus into the absurd position that every Athenian in existence improves the youth while Socrates alone corrupts them.
notes: Speaker: Socrates and Meletus in cross-examination. -- Socrates uses a reductio ad absurdum: by getting Meletus to expand the class of improvers to include all Athenians, he makes the claim that only one person corrupts them obviously absurd. The oath "by the goddess Hera" (ne ten Heran) was considered a women's oath in Athens; Socrates' use of it may be mildly humorous or ironic. The senators (bouleutai) were members of the Boule, the council of 500. The Assembly (ekklesia) was the democratic body of all male citizens. This passage sets up the horse-trainer analogy in 1.17, which will demonstrate that expertise is typically rare, not universal.

narrative: "I am very unfortunate if you are right. But suppose I ask you a question: How about horses? Does one man do them harm and all the world good? Is not the exact opposite the truth? One man is able to do them good, or at least not many;—the trainer of horses, that is to say, does them good, and others who have to do with them rather injure them? Is not that true, Meletus, of horses, or of any other animals? Most assuredly it is; whether you and Anytus say yes or no. Happy indeed would be the condition of youth if they had one corrupter only, and all the rest of the world were their improvers. But you, Meletus, have sufficiently shown that you never had a thought about the young: your carelessness is seen in your not caring about the very things which you bring against me."
refashioned:
"I would be very unfortunate if you were right. But let me ask you this: consider horses. Does one person harm them while everyone else benefits them? Is it not the exact opposite? Only one person -- or very few -- the horse-trainer -- actually does them good. Everyone else who handles them tends to do them harm. Is that not true, Meletus, of horses and all other animals? It most certainly is, whether you and Anytus agree or not.

How fortunate the young would be if they had only one corrupter while all the rest of the world improved them! But you, Meletus, have demonstrated clearly enough that you have never given a moment's thought to the youth. Your carelessness is exposed by your indifference to the very matters you bring against me."
elegant: Consider horses: does one person harm them while everyone else helps? The opposite is true -- only the expert trainer benefits them, while the many do harm. The same applies to youth. Meletus has proven he never thought about the young at all; his carelessness is exposed by his indifference to the very charge he brings.
summary: Socrates uses the horse-trainer analogy to demolish Meletus' claim: in reality, expertise is rare and the many do harm, not good. Only a few can properly train horses or educate youth. Meletus' absurd position proves his indifference to the subject.
distilled: The horse-trainer analogy proves that beneficial expertise is rare, not universal -- demolishing Meletus' claim that everyone improves youth except Socrates and exposing his complete indifference to the subject.
notes: Speaker: Socrates, addressing Meletus. -- The horse-trainer analogy is a classic Socratic argument from craft expertise (techne), used throughout the dialogues: just as training horses requires specialized knowledge, educating youth requires expertise that the many do not possess. This argument appears in similar form in the Crito (47a-48a), where Socrates argues that one should heed expert opinion rather than popular opinion, just as a gymnast follows his trainer rather than the crowd. The mention of Anytus alongside Meletus reminds the jury that the real power behind the prosecution is not the young poet Meletus but the established politician Anytus.

narrative:
"And now, Meletus, I will ask you another question—by Zeus I will: Which is better, to live among bad citizens, or among good ones? Answer, friend, I say; the question is one which may be easily answered. Do not the good do their neighbours good, and the bad do them evil?"

"Certainly."

"And is there anyone who would rather be injured than benefited by those who live with him? Answer, my good friend, the law requires you to answer—does any one like to be injured?"

"Certainly not."

"And when you accuse me of corrupting and deteriorating the youth, do you allege that I corrupt them intentionally or unintentionally?"

"Intentionally, I say."
refashioned:
"Now, Meletus, I will ask you another question -- by Zeus, I will. Is it better to live among good citizens or bad ones? Come, answer -- this is easy. Do good people not benefit their neighbors, and bad people harm them?"

"Certainly."

"Is there anyone who would rather be harmed than helped by those around him? Answer, my good friend -- the law requires you to answer. Does anyone choose to be injured?"

"Certainly not."

"When you accuse me of corrupting and degrading the youth, do you claim I do it intentionally or unintentionally?"

"Intentionally, I say."
elegant: "Is it better to live among good citizens or bad?" Good, says Meletus. "Does anyone prefer to be harmed?" No. "Then do you claim I corrupt the youth intentionally or unintentionally?" Intentionally, Meletus insists -- setting the trap for himself.
summary: Socrates establishes two premises -- that good neighbors benefit us and no one wants to be harmed -- then asks whether the alleged corruption is intentional. Meletus claims it is intentional, walking into Socrates' logical trap.
distilled: Socrates lays a logical trap by establishing that no one wants to be harmed by associates, then gets Meletus to insist the alleged corruption is intentional -- a claim Socrates will show to be self-defeating.
notes: Speaker: Socrates and Meletus. -- This passage sets up a dilemma that Socrates will spring in 1.19. The argument turns on the principle that no one does evil willingly (the Socratic paradox), a doctrine central to the Protagoras (345d-e) and Meno (77b-78b). If Socrates corrupts people intentionally, he would be knowingly creating bad neighbors who would harm him -- which no rational person would do. The legal point about compelled testimony ("the law requires you to answer") reflects Athenian trial procedure where the accused could compel the accuser to respond to questions.

narrative: "But you have just admitted that the good do their neighbours good, and the evil do them evil. Now, is that a truth which your superior wisdom has recognized thus early in life, and am I, at my age, in such darkness and ignorance as not to know that if a man with whom I have to live is corrupted by me, I am very likely to be harmed by him; and yet I corrupt him, and intentionally, too—so you say, although neither I nor any other human being is ever likely to be convinced by you. But either I do not corrupt them, or I corrupt them unintentionally; and on either view of the case you lie. If my offence is unintentional, the law has no cognizance of unintentional offences: you ought to have taken me privately, and warned and admonished me; for if I had been better advised, I should have left off doing what I only did unintentionally—no doubt I should; but you would have nothing to say to me and refused to teach me. And now you bring me up in this court, which is a place not of instruction, but of punishment."
refashioned:
"But you just admitted that good people benefit their neighbors and bad people harm them. Has your superior wisdom grasped this truth at such a young age, while I, at my advanced age, remain so ignorant that I would deliberately corrupt the people I live among -- knowing they would likely harm me in return? You claim I do this intentionally, but no reasonable person would ever believe you.

The reality is this: either I do not corrupt them at all, or I do so unintentionally. Either way, you are lying. If my offense is unintentional, the law does not recognize unintentional offenses as criminal -- you should have come to me privately to warn and instruct me. If I had been properly advised, I would certainly have stopped doing what I was doing without realizing its harm. But you refused to approach me and teach me. Instead, you bring me to this court, which is a place of punishment, not instruction."
elegant: You admitted that bad neighbors harm us. So would I deliberately create bad neighbors for myself? Either I do not corrupt the youth, or I do so unintentionally. If unintentional, the law does not punish unintentional acts -- you should have warned me privately. But you never tried to instruct me. You brought me to a place of punishment, not education.
summary: Socrates springs the trap: if corrupting associates would harm himself, no rational person would do it intentionally. Either he does not corrupt, or does so unintentionally -- and unintentional offenses require private correction, not criminal prosecution.
distilled: Socrates demonstrates that intentional corruption is self-defeating since it harms the corrupter, leaving only two options -- innocence or unintentional error -- neither of which warrants criminal prosecution.
notes: Speaker: Socrates, addressing Meletus. -- This argument exemplifies the Socratic paradox that no one does evil willingly (oudeis hekon hamartanei). If someone corrupts their associates, they create people who will harm them -- something no rational person would choose. The legal distinction between intentional and unintentional harm was recognized in Athenian law: unintentional homicide, for instance, was tried at the Palladium rather than the Areopagus and carried lighter penalties. Socrates' argument that unintentional harm should be addressed through private instruction rather than prosecution reflects his view, expressed in the Gorgias (472d-479e), that punishment should aim at improvement rather than retribution.

narrative:
"It will be very clear to you, Athenians, as I was saying, that Meletus has no care at all, great or small, about the matter. But still I should like to know, Meletus, in what I am affirmed to corrupt the young. I suppose you mean, as I infer from your indictment, that I teach them not to acknowledge the gods which the state acknowledges, but some other new divinities or spiritual agencies in their stead. These are the lessons by which I corrupt the youth, as you say."

"Yes, that I say emphatically."
refashioned:
"It should be clear to you, Athenians, as I have been arguing, that Meletus cares nothing whatsoever about this matter. But I would still like to know, Meletus: in what way exactly do I corrupt the young? I take it from your indictment that you mean I teach them not to believe in the gods the state recognizes, but in other new spiritual beings instead. These are the teachings by which I supposedly corrupt the youth. Is that your claim?"

"Yes, that is exactly what I claim."
elegant: Meletus clearly cares nothing about this. But tell me, Meletus: how exactly do I corrupt the young? From your indictment, I gather you mean I teach them to reject the state's gods in favor of new spiritual beings. "Yes, exactly," Meletus affirms.
summary: Socrates transitions from the corruption charge to the religious charge, getting Meletus to confirm that the alleged corruption consists specifically of teaching youth to reject the city's gods in favor of new spiritual beings.
distilled: Socrates pins Meletus down on the specific nature of the corruption charge: teaching youth to worship new spiritual beings instead of the state-recognized gods.
notes: Speaker: Socrates and Meletus. -- This passage pivots from the youth-corruption charge to the impiety charge, which Socrates treats as the substantive content of the corruption accusation. The "new divinities" (kaina daimonia) likely refers to Socrates' daimonion, the divine sign or inner voice he frequently mentioned. The Euthyphro, set on the steps of the court just before this trial, explores the nature of piety and the relationship between gods and goodness -- questions directly relevant to this charge. The formal charge of introducing new gods was a serious one in Athens; it was reportedly the charge that had driven Anaxagoras from the city decades earlier.

narrative: "Then, by the gods, Meletus, of whom we are speaking, tell me and the court, in somewhat plainer terms, what you mean! for I do not as yet understand whether you affirm that I teach other men to acknowledge some gods, and therefore that I do believe in gods, and am not an entire atheist—this you do not lay to my charge,—but only you say that they are not the same gods which the city recognizes—the charge is that they are different gods. Or, do you mean that I am an atheist simply, and a teacher of atheism?"
refashioned: "Then by the gods, Meletus -- the very gods we are discussing -- tell me and the court more plainly what you mean. I do not yet understand whether you are saying that I teach people to believe in some gods, meaning I am not a complete atheist -- you do not charge me with that -- but only that I recognize different gods from the city's gods. Or are you claiming that I am simply an atheist and teach atheism?"
elegant: "Clarify your charge, Meletus. Are you saying I believe in different gods from the city's -- meaning I am not an atheist but recognize the wrong gods? Or are you claiming I am a complete atheist who teaches atheism?"
summary: Socrates presses Meletus to clarify his charge: does he accuse Socrates of believing in different gods than the state recognizes, or of being a complete atheist? The two claims are contradictory.
distilled: Socrates forces Meletus to choose between two contradictory charges -- believing in different gods or being a complete atheist -- setting up the logical contradiction that will unravel the prosecution's case.
notes: Speaker: Socrates, addressing Meletus. -- This question is strategically brilliant: it forces Meletus into a dilemma. The original indictment charged Socrates with "not believing in the gods the city believes in, but in other new spiritual beings" -- which implies belief in some gods. If Meletus says Socrates believes in different gods, the charge of atheism fails. If Meletus says Socrates is a complete atheist, it contradicts the indictment's reference to "new spiritual beings." Socrates will exploit whichever answer Meletus gives. The irony of swearing "by the gods" while discussing an atheism charge is deliberate Socratic humor. This sets up the demigods argument that Socrates develops through entries 1.22-1.27.

narrative:
"I mean the latter -- that you are a complete atheist."

"What an extraordinary statement! Why do you think so, Meletus? Do you mean that I do not believe in the godhead of the sun or moon, like other men?"

"I assure you, judges, that he does not: for he says that the sun is stone, and the moon earth."
refashioned:
"I mean the latter -- that you are a complete atheist."

"What an extraordinary claim! Why would you think that, Meletus? Are you saying I don't believe the sun and moon are gods, as other people do?"

"I assure you, members of the jury, that he does not. He says the sun is just a rock and the moon is made of earth."
elegant: Meletus declares Socrates a complete atheist. Socrates asks what he means -- does he deny even the divinity of the sun and moon? Meletus claims Socrates teaches the sun is stone and the moon earth.
summary: Under cross-examination, Meletus commits to calling Socrates a total atheist. When pressed, Meletus claims Socrates denies even the divinity of the sun and moon, asserting he teaches they are mere physical matter.
distilled: Meletus overreaches by calling Socrates a complete atheist who denies even the divinity of the sun and moon.
notes: Speaker: Dialogue between Socrates and Meletus. -- The claim that the sun is stone and the moon earth is the cosmology of Anaxagoras of Clazomenae (c. 500-428 BCE), not Socrates -- a confusion Socrates will exploit in 1.23. Aristophanes' Clouds similarly attributes natural philosophy to Socrates. This conflation of Socrates with the pre-Socratic natural philosophers is central to the 'old accusations' discussed in Chapter 1.1. -- Cross-reference: In the Phaedo (97b-99d), Socrates describes reading Anaxagoras and being disappointed by his purely mechanistic explanations, confirming his distance from such views.

narrative:
Friend Meletus, you think that you are accusing Anaxagoras: and you have but a bad opinion of the judges, if you fancy them illiterate to such a degree as not to know that these doctrines are found in the books of Anaxagoras the Clazomenian, which are full of them. And so, forsooth, the youth are said to be taught them by Socrates, when there are not unfrequently exhibitions of them at the theatre (price of admission one drachma at the most); and they might pay their money, and laugh at Socrates if he pretends to father these extraordinary views. And so, Meletus, you really think that I do not believe in any god?

"I swear by Zeus that you believe absolutely in none at all."
refashioned:
My dear Meletus, you seem to think you're prosecuting Anaxagoras. And you must have a very low opinion of this jury if you imagine they're so uneducated as to not know that these doctrines come from the books of Anaxagoras of Clazomenae, which are full of them. So supposedly the young learn these ideas from Socrates, when in fact they can hear them performed at the theater any day for a drachma at most -- and afterward they could laugh at me if I tried to claim these eccentric theories as my own. So tell me, Meletus, do you truly think I believe in no god at all?

"I swear by Zeus that you believe in absolutely none."
elegant: Socrates points out that the doctrines Meletus attributes to him actually come from Anaxagoras, whose ideas are publicly available at the theater for a drachma. The jury knows this. Yet Meletus doubles down, swearing by Zeus that Socrates believes in no gods whatsoever.
summary: Socrates exposes Meletus's confusion by noting the cosmological views attributed to him actually belong to Anaxagoras and are publicly available. Meletus nevertheless swears by Zeus that Socrates is a total atheist.
distilled: Socrates shows the atheism charge actually describes Anaxagoras's publicly known teachings, not his own, but Meletus doubles down regardless.
notes: Speaker: Socrates, with Meletus responding. -- Anaxagoras of Clazomenae (c. 500-428 BCE) was a pre-Socratic philosopher who taught that the sun was a hot stone and the moon earthlike. His books were reportedly available for one drachma. The theatrical reference likely alludes to Aristophanes' Clouds and Euripides' plays, which dramatized Anaxagorean ideas. -- The irony of Meletus swearing by Zeus while accusing Socrates of atheism is pointed. -- Cross-reference: Phaedo 97b-99d for Socrates' own account of reading Anaxagoras. The distinction between Socratic and pre-Socratic philosophy is fundamental to understanding the charges.

narrative:
Nobody will believe you, Meletus, and I am pretty sure that you do not believe yourself. I cannot help thinking, men of Athens, that Meletus is reckless and impudent, and that he has written this indictment in a spirit of mere wantonness and youthful bravado. Has he not compounded a riddle, thinking to try me? He said to himself: "I shall see whether the wise Socrates will discover my facetious contradiction, or whether I shall be able to deceive him and the rest of them." For he certainly does appear to me to contradict himself in the indictment as much as if he said that Socrates is guilty of not believing in the gods, and yet of believing in them -- but this is not like a person who is in earnest.

I should like you, O men of Athens, to join me in examining what I conceive to be his inconsistency; and do you, Meletus, answer. And I must remind the audience of my request that they would not make a disturbance if I speak in my accustomed manner:
refashioned:
No one will believe you, Meletus, and I'm fairly certain you don't believe it yourself. I can't help but think, men of Athens, that Meletus is being reckless and brazen, and that he drafted this indictment out of sheer irresponsibility and youthful arrogance. Hasn't he constructed a kind of riddle to test me? He thought to himself: "Let's see whether the clever Socrates can spot my amusing contradiction, or whether I can fool him and everyone else." Because he clearly contradicts himself in the indictment -- it's as if he wrote that Socrates is guilty of not believing in the gods, and also of believing in them. That's not how a serious person behaves.

I'd like you, men of Athens, to join me in examining what I believe is his inconsistency. And you, Meletus -- answer my questions. And I must remind the audience of my earlier request: please don't cause a disturbance when I speak in my usual manner.
