elegant: Socrates declares that no one believes Meletus's charge, not even Meletus himself. The indictment is a riddle -- a self-contradicting joke. It accuses Socrates both of not believing in gods and of believing in them. Socrates invites the jury to examine this inconsistency and asks Meletus to answer his questions.
summary: Socrates accuses Meletus of constructing a self-contradictory indictment as a kind of riddle or test. The charge simultaneously accuses Socrates of atheism and of believing in new divinities. He invites the jury to examine this inconsistency.
distilled: Socrates exposes the indictment's central contradiction: it charges him with both atheism and belief in new divinities simultaneously.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- This passage sets up the formal logical trap (1.25-1.27) that will demolish the atheism charge. The indictment's contradiction -- accusing Socrates of not believing in gods while also introducing new spiritual beings -- is the crux of the elenctic cross-examination. -- Socrates' characterization of Meletus as youthful and reckless echoes the portrait in the Euthyphro (2b), where Meletus is described as young and unknown. -- Cross-reference: The method of exposing contradictions in an interlocutor's position is the hallmark of the Socratic elenchus, demonstrated throughout the early dialogues (Euthyphro, Gorgias, Meno).

narrative:
Did ever man, Meletus, believe in the existence of human things, and not of human beings?...I wish, men of Athens, that he would answer, and not be always trying to get up an interruption. Did ever any man believe in horsemanship, and not in horses? or in flute-playing, and not in flute-players? No, my friend; I will answer to you and to the court, as you refuse to answer for yourself. There is no man who ever did. But now please to answer the next question: Can a man believe in spiritual and divine agencies, and not in spirits or demigods?

"He cannot."
refashioned:
Tell me, Meletus -- has any man ever believed in human activities but not in the existence of human beings? I wish, men of Athens, that he would just answer instead of constantly trying to create interruptions. Has anyone ever believed in horsemanship but not in horses? Or in flute-playing but not in flute-players? Of course not. Since you refuse to answer, I'll answer for you and the court: no one ever has. But now answer this next question: Can a person believe in spiritual and divine activities without believing in spirits or demigods?

"He cannot."
elegant: Socrates builds his logical trap through analogy: no one believes in horsemanship without horses, or flute-playing without flute-players. By the same logic, one cannot believe in spiritual activities without believing in spirits. Meletus concedes the point.
summary: Socrates uses a series of analogies to establish that belief in activities necessarily implies belief in the agents who perform them. No one believes in horsemanship without horses. Meletus concedes that believing in spiritual agencies requires believing in spirits or demigods.
distilled: Through simple analogies, Socrates forces Meletus to admit that belief in spiritual activities logically requires belief in spiritual beings.
notes: Speaker: Dialogue between Socrates and Meletus. -- This is classic Socratic elenchus: using the interlocutor's own admissions to build toward a contradiction. The analogical reasoning (horsemanship implies horses, flute-playing implies flute-players) is characteristically Socratic -- drawing on everyday examples to establish philosophical points. -- Cross-reference: Similar analogical reasoning appears in the Euthyphro (the question of what piety is), and the Meno (the slave boy demonstration). The method of drawing admissions from a reluctant interlocutor is also prominent in the Gorgias.

narrative:
How lucky I am to have extracted that answer, by the assistance of the court! But then you swear in the indictment that I teach and believe in divine or spiritual agencies (new or old, no matter for that); at any rate, I believe in spiritual agencies, -- so you say and swear in the affidavit; and yet if I believe in divine beings, how can I help believing in spirits or demigods; -- must I not? To be sure I must; and therefore I may assume that your silence gives consent. Now what are spirits or demigods? Are they not either gods or the sons of gods?

"Certainly they are."
refashioned:
How fortunate that I managed to extract that answer, with the court's help! Now then -- you swear in the indictment that I teach and believe in divine or spiritual agencies, whether new or old. So at the very least, you affirm under oath that I believe in spiritual agencies. But if I believe in divine beings, how can I possibly not believe in spirits or demigods? Of course I must. I'll take your silence as agreement. Now, what are spirits or demigods? Aren't they either gods themselves or the children of gods?

"They certainly are."
elegant: Socrates springs the trap: the indictment itself swears he believes in spiritual agencies. But spiritual agencies imply spirits or demigods. And demigods are by definition gods or sons of gods. Meletus agrees to each step.
summary: Socrates closes the logical trap by pointing out that the indictment itself swears he believes in spiritual agencies, which necessarily imply demigods, which are either gods or children of gods. Meletus agrees at every step, sealing his own contradiction.
distilled: The indictment's own sworn claim that Socrates believes in spiritual agencies logically entails his belief in gods -- refuting the atheism charge.
notes: Speaker: Dialogue between Socrates and Meletus. -- The argument is a syllogism: (1) Socrates believes in spiritual agencies (from the indictment). (2) Spiritual agencies imply spirits/demigods (from Meletus's admission in 1.25). (3) Demigods are gods or sons of gods (from Meletus's admission here). Therefore Socrates believes in gods. The atheism charge self-destructs. -- The concept of daimonia (spiritual agencies/demigods) is central. Socrates' own daimonion (divine sign) is discussed in 1.36. -- Cross-reference: The nature of daimones is discussed in the Symposium (202d-203a), where Diotima describes Eros as a daimon mediating between gods and mortals.

narrative: But this is what I call the facetious riddle invented by you: the demigods or spirits are gods, and you say first that I do not believe in gods, and then again that I do believe in gods; that is, if I believe in demigods. For if the demigods are the illegitimate sons of gods, whether by the nymphs or by any other mothers, of whom they are said to be the sons -- what human being will ever believe that there are no gods if they are the sons of gods? You might as well affirm the existence of mules, and deny that of horses and asses. Such nonsense, Meletus, could only have been intended by you to make trial of me. You have put this into the indictment because you had nothing real of which to accuse me. But no one who has a particle of understanding will ever be convinced by you that the same men can believe in divine and superhuman things, and yet not believe that there are gods and demigods and heroes.
refashioned: And this is the absurd riddle you've constructed: demigods are gods or children of gods, yet you say first that I don't believe in gods, and then that I do -- since I believe in demigods. If demigods are the children of gods, whether by nymphs or any other mothers they're said to have, what person could possibly believe there are no gods while accepting the existence of their children? That would be like affirming the existence of mules while denying the existence of horses and donkeys. This kind of nonsense, Meletus, can only have been your attempt to test me. You put this in the indictment because you had nothing real to charge me with. No one with the slightest intelligence will ever be convinced that a person can believe in divine and superhuman things while simultaneously not believing in gods, demigods, and heroes.
elegant: Socrates delivers the final blow: believing in demigods while denying gods is like believing in mules while denying horses and donkeys. The indictment refutes itself. Meletus had nothing real to charge, so he constructed this logical absurdity.
summary: Socrates completes his demolition of the atheism charge with a vivid analogy: denying gods while accepting demigods is like denying horses and donkeys while accepting mules. He concludes the charge was fabricated because Meletus had nothing real to accuse him of.
distilled: Believing in demigods while denying gods is as absurd as believing in mules while denying horses and donkeys -- the charge refutes itself.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- This completes the formal elenctic refutation of the atheism charge (1.22-1.27). The mule analogy is characteristically Socratic -- using a homely comparison to make a logical point devastatingly clear. -- The mention of demigods as 'illegitimate sons of gods by nymphs' refers to Greek mythological tradition. Heroes (mentioned at the end) were typically demigods -- humans of divine parentage. -- Cross-reference: The relationship between gods, demigods, and heroes is discussed in the Symposium (202e). The charge of atheism and its refutation should be read alongside the Euthyphro, where Socrates explores the nature of piety with the very man who may have inspired Meletus's religious charges.

narrative: I have said enough in answer to the charge of Meletus: any elaborate defence is unnecessary, but I know only too well how many are the enmities which I have incurred, and this is what will be my destruction if I am destroyed; -- not Meletus, nor yet Anytus, but the envy and detraction of the world, which has been the death of many good men, and will probably be the death of many more; there is no danger of my being the last of them.
refashioned: I've said enough to answer Meletus's charges -- no elaborate defense is needed. But I know all too well how many enemies I've made, and this is what will destroy me if anything does -- not Meletus, not even Anytus, but the envy and hostility of the crowd, which has been the death of many good people and will likely be the death of many more. There's no chance I'll be the last.
elegant: The formal charges are answered. What truly threatens Socrates is not Meletus or Anytus but the envy and resentment of the many -- a force that has destroyed good people before and will again.
summary: Having refuted Meletus's charges, Socrates acknowledges that his real danger comes not from his accusers but from the widespread envy and hostility his philosophical mission has generated.
distilled: Socrates' true enemy is not Meletus or Anytus but the accumulated envy and resentment of the crowd.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- This transitional passage marks the shift from Chapter 1.2 (cross-examination of Meletus) to Chapter 1.3 (the philosopher's duty and character defense). Socrates names the real threat: popular resentment. -- The observation that envy destroys good people echoes a common Greek theme (phthonos). -- Cross-reference: In the Gorgias (521c-522a), Socrates predicts he would be convicted if tried, comparing himself to a doctor prosecuted by a pastry chef before a jury of children. The Crito (44d-46a) shows Socrates' indifference to popular opinion after conviction.

narrative: Some one will say: "And are you not ashamed, Socrates, of a course of life which is likely to bring you to an untimely end?" To him I may fairly answer: "There you are mistaken: a man who is good for anything ought not to calculate the chance of living or dying; he ought only to consider whether in doing anything he is doing right or wrong -- acting the part of a good man or of a bad." Whereas, upon your view, the heroes who fell at Troy were not good for much, and the son of Thetis above all, who altogether despised danger in comparison with disgrace; and when he was so eager to slay Hector, his goddess mother said to him, that if he avenged his companion Patroclus, and slew Hector, he would die himself -- "Fate," she said, in these or the like words, "waits for you next after Hector;" he, receiving this warning, utterly despised danger and death, and instead of fearing them, feared rather to live in dishonour, and not to avenge his friend. "Let me die forthwith," he replies, "and be avenged of my enemy, rather than abide here by the beaked ships, a laughing-stock and a burden of the earth." Had Achilles any thought of death and danger? For wherever a man's place is, whether the place which he has chosen or that in which he has been placed by a commander, there he ought to remain in the hour of danger; he should not think of death or of anything but of disgrace. And this, O men of Athens, is a true saying.
refashioned:
Someone might say: "Aren't you ashamed, Socrates, of living a life that's likely to get you killed?" To that I can fairly answer: "You're mistaken. A person of any worth shouldn't calculate their chances of living or dying. The only question is whether what they're doing is right or wrong -- whether they're acting as a good person or a bad one."

By your logic, the heroes who died at Troy were worthless, especially Achilles, son of Thetis, who completely despised danger compared to disgrace. When he burned to avenge Patroclus and kill Hector, his goddess mother warned him that his own death would follow Hector's. "Fate waits for you next after Hector," she told him. Hearing this, he utterly dismissed the danger. What he feared was not death but living in dishonor without avenging his friend. "Let me die immediately and have my revenge," he replied, "rather than sit here by the ships, a joke and a burden on the earth."

Did Achilles worry about death and danger? No. Wherever a person's post is -- whether chosen by themselves or assigned by a commander -- that is where they must stand in the hour of danger. They should think not of death but only of disgrace. And this, men of Athens, is the truth.
elegant: A worthy person doesn't calculate their odds of survival -- only whether they're acting rightly. Achilles chose death over dishonor when he avenged Patroclus, knowing it meant his own end. A person must hold their post -- whether self-chosen or assigned -- and fear only disgrace, never death.
summary: Socrates argues that a good person should never weigh survival against doing right, citing Achilles as his model. Achilles chose certain death over the dishonor of failing to avenge Patroclus. Wherever one's post is, one must stand firm and fear only disgrace.
distilled: A person of worth calculates only right and wrong, never survival -- as Achilles chose death over dishonor at Troy.
notes: Speaker: Socrates, quoting a hypothetical objector and paraphrasing Homer's Iliad (18.94-104). -- The Achilles parallel is central to Socrates' self-understanding: philosophy is his post, assigned by God, just as a soldier's post is assigned by a commander. -- Thetis is Achilles' divine mother, a sea-nymph. Patroclus was Achilles' closest companion. Hector was the Trojan champion. -- Cross-reference: The duty to hold one's post recurs in 1.30, where Socrates compares his philosophical mission to his military service at Potidaea, Amphipolis, and Delium. In the Crito (51b-c), Socrates uses a similar argument about not abandoning one's station. In the Symposium (219e-221b), Alcibiades describes Socrates' extraordinary courage at Potidaea and Delium.

narrative: Strange, indeed, would be my conduct, O men of Athens, if I who, when I was ordered by the generals whom you chose to command me at Potidaea and Amphipolis and Delium, remained where they placed me, like any other man, facing death -- if now, when, as I conceive and imagine, God orders me to fulfil the philosopher's mission of searching into myself and other men, I were to desert my post through fear of death, or any other fear; that would indeed be strange, and I might justly be arraigned in court for denying the existence of the gods, if I disobeyed the oracle because I was afraid of death, fancying that I was wise when I was not wise.
refashioned: My behavior would be truly strange, men of Athens, if I -- who held my ground when the generals you elected ordered me to fight at Potidaea, Amphipolis, and Delium, standing my post like any other soldier and facing death -- were now to abandon my post out of fear of death or anything else, when God himself orders me to fulfill the philosopher's mission of examining myself and others. That would indeed be bizarre, and you could rightly bring me to court for denying the gods -- because I would be disobeying the oracle out of fear of death, imagining myself wise when I was not.
elegant: Socrates held his ground under military orders at Potidaea, Amphipolis, and Delium. To desert his philosophical post now -- when God commands it -- would be the real impiety. Abandoning the oracle's mission out of fear would truly prove he doesn't believe in the gods.
summary: Socrates draws a direct parallel between his military service and his philosophical mission. Having held his post at Potidaea, Amphipolis, and Delium under human generals, it would be absurd to desert the post God assigned him. Abandoning philosophy out of fear would be the true act of impiety.
distilled: Having held his military post facing death at Potidaea, Amphipolis, and Delium, deserting his God-given philosophical post would be the real impiety.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- Potidaea (432 BCE): Athenian siege of a Corinthian colony; one of the opening conflicts of the Peloponnesian War. Amphipolis (422 BCE): Athenian defeat in Thrace. Delium (424 BCE): Athenian defeat in Boeotia. Socrates served as a hoplite (infantry soldier) in all three. -- The argument ingeniously turns the impiety charge on its head: abandoning philosophy would be the truly impious act, since philosophy is God's command via the Delphic oracle. -- Cross-reference: Alcibiades describes Socrates' remarkable courage at Potidaea and Delium in the Symposium (219e-221c), including saving Alcibiades' life and his extraordinary composure during the retreat from Delium. The Laches (181b) also references his conduct at Delium.

narrative: For the fear of death is indeed the pretence of wisdom, and not real wisdom, being a pretence of knowing the unknown; and no one knows whether death, which men in their fear apprehend to be the greatest evil, may not be the greatest good. Is not this ignorance of a disgraceful sort, the ignorance which is the conceit that a man knows what he does not know? And in this respect only I believe myself to differ from men in general, and may perhaps claim to be wiser than they are: -- that whereas I know but little of the world below, I do not suppose that I know: but I do know that injustice and disobedience to a better, whether God or man, is evil and dishonourable, and I will never fear or avoid a possible good rather than a certain evil.
refashioned:
Fearing death is a pretense of wisdom, not real wisdom -- it's pretending to know what we don't know. No one knows whether death, which people fearfully assume to be the greatest evil, might actually be the greatest good. Isn't this a particularly shameful kind of ignorance -- the conceit of thinking you know what you don't?

In this one respect I may differ from most people, and perhaps claim to be wiser: I know very little about what comes after death, and I don't pretend otherwise. But I do know that injustice and disobedience to a superior -- whether God or human -- is evil and dishonorable. I will never flee from what might be good in order to avoid what I know for certain is evil.
elegant: Fearing death is false wisdom -- pretending to know the unknown. Death might be the greatest good. Socrates' only claim to wisdom: he doesn't pretend to know what he doesn't. He does know that injustice and disobedience are evil. He won't flee a possible good to avoid a certain evil.
summary: Socrates argues that fearing death is a form of false wisdom -- pretending to know something unknowable. His distinctive wisdom is knowing what he doesn't know. He knows injustice is evil; he doesn't know death is evil. So he will never choose certain evil (injustice) to avoid possible good (death).
distilled: Fearing death is pretending to know the unknown; the only certain evil is injustice and disobedience, and Socrates will never choose that over death.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- This passage encapsulates several core Socratic doctrines: (1) the epistemological humility from the oracle story -- knowing that one doesn't know; (2) the equation of virtue with knowledge; (3) the asymmetry between certain knowledge of moral evil and uncertain knowledge of death. -- The phrase 'possible good rather than a certain evil' frames the entire Apology's ethical logic. -- Cross-reference: The Phaedo (63b-69e) develops the philosopher's relationship to death at length. The Crito (48c-d) reaffirms that the important thing is not living but living well. The Gorgias (469b-479e) argues it is worse to do injustice than to suffer it.

narrative: And therefore if you let me go now, and are not convinced by Anytus, who said that since I had been prosecuted I must be put to death; (or if not that I ought never to have been prosecuted at all); and that if I escape now, your sons will all be utterly ruined by listening to my words -- if you say to me, "Socrates, this time we will not mind Anytus, and you shall be let off, but upon one condition, that you are not to enquire and speculate in this way any more, and that if you are caught doing so again you shall die;" -- if this was the condition on which you let me go, I should reply: "Men of Athens, I honour and love you; but I shall obey God rather than you, and while I have life and strength I shall never cease from the practice and teaching of philosophy, exhorting any one whom I meet and saying to him after my manner: 'You, my friend, -- a citizen of the great and mighty and wise city of Athens, -- are you not ashamed of heaping up the greatest amount of money and honour and reputation, and caring so little about wisdom and truth and the greatest improvement of the soul, which you never regard or heed at all?'" And if the person with whom I am arguing, says: "Yes, but I do care;" then I do not leave him or let him go at once; but I proceed to interrogate and examine and cross-examine him, and if I think that he has no virtue in him, but only says that he has, I reproach him with undervaluing the greater, and overvaluing the less.
refashioned:
So suppose you decide to release me, disregarding Anytus -- who argued that either I should never have been prosecuted or, having been prosecuted, must certainly be executed, and who warned that if I go free, your sons will be completely corrupted by my teachings. Suppose you say to me: "Socrates, we'll ignore Anytus this time and let you go -- but on one condition: you must stop this questioning and philosophical investigation. If you're caught doing it again, you will die."

If those were your terms, I would answer: "Men of Athens, I respect and love you, but I will obey God rather than you. As long as I breathe and have the strength, I will never stop practicing and teaching philosophy. I will keep approaching people in my usual way and saying: 'You there -- a citizen of Athens, this great, powerful, and renowned city -- aren't you ashamed of devoting yourself to accumulating as much money, reputation, and honors as possible, while giving no thought to wisdom, truth, and the improvement of your soul?'"

And if someone responds, "But I do care about those things," I don't just let him go. I question him, examine him, cross-examine him. And if I find he has no real virtue but only claims to, I reproach him for valuing what matters less over what matters most.
elegant: Even if offered freedom on the condition he stop philosophizing, Socrates would refuse. He will obey God over Athens. He will never stop confronting citizens: Are you not ashamed of pursuing wealth and reputation while neglecting your soul? If they claim virtue, he examines them -- and exposes the pretense.
summary: Socrates declares that even if Athens offered to acquit him on condition he stop philosophizing, he would refuse. He will obey God rather than the city, continuing to challenge every citizen to care for wisdom and their soul above wealth and reputation.
distilled: Socrates would refuse freedom itself if it meant abandoning philosophy -- he will obey God rather than Athens and never stop examining souls.
notes: Speaker: Socrates, with hypothetical dialogue. -- 'I shall obey God rather than you' is one of the most famous declarations in Western philosophy, establishing the principle of individual conscience against state authority. -- Anytus was likely the driving force behind the prosecution, a wealthy democratic politician. Plutarch reports Anytus was later exiled for his role in Socrates' death. -- Cross-reference: The Crito (48b-54d) dramatizes the tension between obedience to the state and higher principles. The soul's priority over the body echoes the Phaedo (64c-67b). The method described -- exhorting, examining, cross-examining -- is the Socratic elenchus seen throughout the early dialogues.

narrative: And I shall repeat the same words to every one whom I meet, young and old, citizen and alien, but especially to the citizens, inasmuch as they are my brethren. For know that this is the command of God; and I believe that no greater good has ever happened in the state than my service to the God. For I do nothing but go about persuading you all, old and young alike, not to take thought for your persons or your properties, but first and chiefly to care about the greatest improvement of the soul. I tell you that virtue is not given by money, but that from virtue comes money and every other good of man, public as well as private. This is my teaching, and if this is the doctrine which corrupts the youth, I am a mischievous person. But if any one says that this is not my teaching, he is speaking an untruth. Wherefore, O men of Athens, I say to you, do as Anytus bids or not as Anytus bids, and either acquit me or not; but whichever you do, understand that I shall never alter my ways, not even if I have to die many times.
refashioned:
I will say the same things to everyone I meet -- young and old, citizen and foreigner -- but especially to my fellow Athenians, since you are my own people. Understand: this is God's command. And I believe no greater good has ever come to this city than my service to God. All I do is go around urging every one of you, young and old, not to prioritize your bodies or your possessions, but first and above all to care about the improvement of your soul.

I tell you: virtue does not come from money. Rather, from virtue comes money and every other good thing, both for individuals and for the city. This is my teaching. If this doctrine corrupts the youth, then I suppose I am a dangerous person. But if anyone claims this is not what I teach, they are lying.

Therefore, men of Athens, I say to you: follow Anytus's advice or don't. Acquit me or convict me. But understand this -- I will never change my ways, not even if I have to die many times over.
elegant: Socrates' message to everyone: care for the soul above body and property. Virtue doesn't come from money; money and all good things come from virtue. This is God's command. If this teaching corrupts, then Socrates is guilty. But he will never change, regardless of the verdict -- not even if he must die many times.
summary: Socrates summarizes his core teaching: prioritize the soul over body and property, because virtue produces all other goods, not the reverse. He declares this is God's command and the greatest service to Athens. He will never alter this mission regardless of the verdict.
distilled: Virtue produces all good things, not money -- and Socrates will never stop teaching this, even if he must die for it many times over.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- 'Virtue is not given by money, but from virtue comes money and every other good' is a cornerstone of Socratic ethics. This inverts the conventional Greek assumption that wealth enables virtue (through leisure, education, etc.). -- The willingness to die 'many times' echoes the Achilles parallel in 1.29. -- Cross-reference: The priority of soul over body is central to the Phaedo (64c-67b). The thesis that virtue produces all goods connects to the Meno (87d-89a), where Socrates argues virtue is the only unconditional good. The Gorgias (467-468) argues that wealth and power without wisdom are useless.

narrative: Men of Athens, do not interrupt, but hear me; there was an understanding between us that you should hear me to the end: I have something more to say, at which you may be inclined to cry out; but I believe that to hear me will be good for you, and therefore I beg that you will not cry out. I would have you know, that if you kill such an one as I am, you will injure yourselves more than you will injure me. Nothing will injure me, not Meletus nor yet Anytus -- they cannot, for a bad man is not permitted to injure a better than himself. I do not deny that Anytus may, perhaps, kill him, or drive him into exile, or deprive him of civil rights; and he may imagine, and others may imagine, that he is inflicting a great injury upon him: but there I do not agree. For the evil of doing as he is doing -- the evil of unjustly taking away the life of another -- is greater far.
refashioned:
Men of Athens, don't interrupt -- hear me out. We had an agreement that you would listen to the end. What I'm about to say may make you want to shout, but I believe hearing it will benefit you, so I ask you not to.

Know this: if you kill someone like me, you will harm yourselves far more than you harm me. Nothing can truly injure me -- not Meletus, not Anytus. They don't have the power. A worse person is not permitted to injure a better one. Anytus might have me killed, or exiled, or stripped of my civil rights. He and others might imagine this inflicts a great injury. But I disagree. The true evil -- the far greater harm -- lies in doing what he is doing: unjustly taking another person's life.
elegant: Killing Socrates will harm Athens more than it harms him. A worse person cannot truly injure a better one. Anytus may kill, exile, or disenfranchise him, but the real evil lies with the one who unjustly takes a life, not the one who loses it.
summary: Socrates warns Athens that executing him will injure the city more than it injures him. A morally inferior person cannot truly harm a morally superior one. The real evil belongs to whoever unjustly takes another's life, not to the victim.
distilled: Killing Socrates harms Athens more than it harms him, because the true evil belongs to the one who commits injustice, not the one who suffers it.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- The doctrine that the wrongdoer is harmed more than the victim is a foundational Socratic-Platonic principle. It follows from the premise that the soul matters more than the body: injustice damages the perpetrator's soul, while death merely affects the body. -- Cross-reference: This principle is argued at length in the Gorgias (469b-479e), where Socrates defends the thesis that doing injustice is worse than suffering it, and that the unjust person is the most miserable. The Crito (49a-d) reaffirms that one must never do wrong, even in return for wrong. The Republic (Book I, 335b-e) argues that harming others makes them worse.

narrative: And now, Athenians, I am not going to argue for my own sake, as you may think, but for yours, that you may not sin against the God by condemning me, who am his gift to you. For if you kill me you will not easily find a successor to me, who, if I may use such a ludicrous figure of speech, am a sort of gadfly, given to the state by God; and the state is a great and noble steed who is tardy in his motions owing to his very size, and requires to be stirred into life. I am that gadfly which God has attached to the state, and all day long and in all places am always fastening upon you, arousing and persuading and reproaching you. You will not easily find another like me, and therefore I would advise you to spare me. I dare say that you may feel out of temper (like a person who is suddenly awakened from sleep), and you think that you might easily strike me dead as Anytus advises, and then you would sleep on for the remainder of your lives, unless God in his care of you sent you another gadfly. When I say that I am given to you by God, the proof of my mission is this: -- if I had been like other men, I should not have neglected all my own concerns or patiently seen the neglect of them during all these years, and have been doing yours, coming to you individually like a father or elder brother, exhorting you to regard virtue; such conduct, I say, would be unlike human nature. If I had gained anything, or if my exhortations had been paid, there would have been some sense in my doing so; but now, as you will perceive, not even the impudence of my accusers dares to say that I have ever exacted or sought pay of any one; of that they have no witness. And I have a sufficient witness to the truth of what I say -- my poverty.
refashioned:
And now, Athenians, I argue not for my own sake but for yours -- so that you don't sin against God by condemning me, his gift to you. If you kill me, you won't easily find a replacement. To use a somewhat ridiculous metaphor: I am a kind of gadfly, attached to the state by God. The state is like a great, noble horse that has grown sluggish because of its very size and needs to be stung into wakefulness. I am that gadfly -- fastening onto you all day, everywhere, rousing you, persuading you, reproaching you.

You won't easily find another like me, so I'd advise you to spare me. I expect you feel irritated, like someone jolted awake from sleep, and you think you could easily swat me dead, as Anytus recommends. Then you could go back to sleeping for the rest of your lives -- unless God, in his care for you, sent another gadfly.

That I am truly sent by God, here is the proof: no ordinary person would have neglected all their own affairs for years, patiently watching them deteriorate, while devoting themselves to yours -- coming to each of you individually like a father or older brother, urging you to care about virtue. That behavior defies human nature. If I had profited from it, or been paid for my advice, it would make some sense. But as you can see, not even my shameless accusers dare claim I ever charged anyone a fee. They have no evidence of that. And I have a perfectly good witness to the truth of what I say: my poverty.
elegant: Socrates is God's gadfly, stinging a great but sluggish city into wakefulness. Kill him and Athens sleeps on -- unless God sends another. Proof of his divine mission: no sane person would neglect their own life for years to improve others for free. His poverty is his witness.
summary: Socrates presents his famous gadfly metaphor: he is God's gift to Athens, stinging the great but sluggish city into moral wakefulness. His proof of divine mission is that no rational person would sacrifice their own welfare for years to help others without pay. His poverty testifies to his sincerity.
distilled: Socrates is God's gadfly -- stinging sluggish Athens awake -- and his lifelong poverty proves the mission is divine, not self-serving.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- The gadfly (myops) metaphor is among the most famous images in Western philosophy. The 'great and noble steed' represents the Athenian state. -- Socrates' poverty is well attested: Xenophon (Oeconomicus 2.3) mentions his estate was worth about five minae. -- Cross-reference: Socrates' role as a gift to the city echoes the Delphic oracle narrative in 1.7-1.11. His poverty is also mentioned in the Symposium (174a, 219e-220a). The gadfly image anticipates the more systematic political philosophy of the Republic, where philosopher-kings are reluctant rulers who serve the state without personal ambition.

narrative: Some one may wonder why I go about in private giving advice and busying myself with the concerns of others, but do not venture to come forward in public and advise the state. I will tell you why. You have heard me speak at sundry times and in divers places of an oracle or sign which comes to me, and is the divinity which Meletus ridicules in the indictment. This sign, which is a kind of voice, first began to come to me when I was a child; it always forbids but never commands me to do anything which I am going to do. This is what deters me from being a politician. And rightly, as I think. For I am certain, O men of Athens, that if I had engaged in politics, I should have perished long ago, and done no good either to you or to myself. And do not be offended at my telling you the truth: for the truth is, that no man who goes to war with you or any other multitude, honestly striving against the many lawless and unrighteous deeds which are done in a state, will save his life; he who will fight for the right, if he would live even for a brief space, must have a private station and not a public one.
refashioned:
You might wonder why I go around giving advice privately, involving myself in other people's affairs, yet never step forward publicly to advise the state. I'll tell you why.

You've heard me mention at various times and places a kind of oracle or sign that comes to me -- the divine voice that Meletus mocks in his indictment. This sign, a sort of inner voice, first came to me in childhood. It only ever warns me away from things; it never tells me what to do. This is what has kept me out of politics. And rightly so, I believe.

I am certain, men of Athens, that if I had gone into politics, I would have been killed long ago and done no good whatsoever to either you or myself. Don't be offended by the truth, but the truth is this: no person who honestly opposes the many lawless and unjust acts committed in any state -- who genuinely fights the crowd -- will survive. Anyone who truly fights for justice, if they want to live even a little while, must remain in private life, not public office.
elegant: Socrates' divine sign -- a voice since childhood that warns but never commands -- has kept him from politics. Rightly so: anyone who honestly opposes the crowd's injustices will be destroyed. Fighting for justice requires a private station, not a public one.
summary: Socrates explains why he has stayed out of politics despite his concern for virtue: his divine sign warned him away, and rightly so. Anyone who honestly fights the crowd's injustices in public life will be killed. Justice requires a private station.
distilled: Socrates' divine sign kept him from politics because anyone who honestly opposes the crowd's injustice in public life will be destroyed.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- The daimonion (divine sign/voice) is one of the most distinctive features of Socrates' life. It appears throughout Plato's dialogues as a purely prohibitive voice -- it warns Socrates away from actions but never positively commands. This is the 'new divinity' Meletus references in the indictment. -- The political observation is strikingly realistic: Athenian democracy could be ruthless toward dissenters. -- Cross-reference: The daimonion appears in the Phaedrus (242b-c), Euthydemus (272e), Republic (496c), and Theaetetus (151a). Xenophon's Memorabilia (1.1.2-5) also discusses it. The claim that a just person cannot survive in politics is echoed in Republic (496c-e, 517a). The examples of Arginusae and Leon of Salamis (1.37) will illustrate this claim concretely.

narrative: I can give you convincing evidence of what I say, not words only, but what you value far more -- actions. Let me relate to you a passage of my own life which will prove to you that I should never have yielded to injustice from any fear of death, and that "as I should have refused to yield" I must have died at once. I will tell you a tale of the courts, not very interesting perhaps, but nevertheless true. The only office of state which I ever held, O men of Athens, was that of senator: the tribe Antiochis, which is my tribe, had the presidency at the trial of the generals who had not taken up the bodies of the slain after the battle of Arginusae; and you proposed to try them in a body, contrary to law, as you all thought afterwards; but at the time I was the only one of the Prytanes who was opposed to the illegality, and I gave my vote against you; and when the orators threatened to impeach and arrest me, and you called and shouted, I made up my mind that I would run the risk, having law and justice with me, rather than take part in your injustice because I feared imprisonment and death. This happened in the days of the democracy. But when the oligarchy of the Thirty was in power, they sent for me and four others into the rotunda, and bade us bring Leon the Salaminian from Salamis, as they wanted to put him to death. This was a specimen of the sort of commands which they were always giving with the view of implicating as many as possible in their crimes; and then I showed, not in word only but in deed, that, if I may be allowed to use such an expression, I cared not a straw for death, and that my great and only care was lest I should do an unrighteous or unholy thing. For the strong arm of that oppressive power did not frighten me into doing wrong; and when we came out of the rotunda the other four went to Salamis and fetched Leon, but I went quietly home. For which I might have lost my life, had not the power of the Thirty shortly afterwards come to an end. And many will witness to my words.
refashioned:
I can offer you convincing evidence -- not just words, but what you value far more: actions. Let me tell you an episode from my own life that proves I would never yield to injustice from fear of death, and that my refusal to yield would have meant my immediate death.

I'll tell you a story from the courts -- perhaps not thrilling, but true. The only public office I ever held, men of Athens, was senator. My tribe, Antiochis, held the presidency during the trial of the generals who failed to recover the dead after the Battle of Arginusae. You proposed to try them collectively, which was illegal -- as you all later acknowledged. But at the time, I was the only member of the Prytaneis who opposed this illegality. I voted against you. When the orators threatened to impeach and arrest me, and the crowd roared for my head, I decided I would rather face the danger with law and justice on my side than participate in your injustice out of fear of prison or death.

That was under the democracy. Under the oligarchy of the Thirty, they summoned me and four others to the Tholos and ordered us to bring Leon of Salamis from Salamis so they could execute him. This was typical of their methods -- implicating as many people as possible in their crimes. Then I showed, not in words but in action, that I cared nothing for death. My only concern was to avoid doing anything unjust or unholy. The brutal power of that oppressive regime did not frighten me into wrongdoing. When we left the Tholos, the other four went to Salamis and arrested Leon. I simply went home.

I might have been killed for this, had the Thirty's power not collapsed shortly afterward. Many here can testify to the truth of this.
elegant: Socrates offers two proofs from his own life. Under the democracy, he alone opposed the illegal mass trial of the Arginusae generals, despite threats of arrest. Under the Thirty Tyrants, ordered to help arrest Leon of Salamis for execution, he simply went home while the other four obeyed. In both cases, he chose justice over survival.
summary: Socrates provides two concrete examples of choosing justice over self-preservation. Under the democracy, he alone opposed the illegal collective trial of the Arginusae generals. Under the Thirty Tyrants, he refused to arrest Leon of Salamis for execution, going home instead while others complied. Both acts could have cost him his life.
distilled: Socrates proved his principles twice: defying the democratic mob at the Arginusae trial, and defying the Thirty Tyrants by refusing to arrest Leon of Salamis.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- Battle of Arginusae (406 BCE): Athenian naval victory, but the generals were charged with failing to recover the dead and survivors due to a storm. The Assembly illegally voted to try them collectively rather than individually. Six generals were executed. This was later recognized as a grave injustice. -- The Thirty Tyrants (404-403 BCE): An oligarchic regime installed after Athens' defeat in the Peloponnesian War, led by Critias and Charicles. They ruled through terror and killed approximately 1,500 Athenians. -- Leon of Salamis: An innocent Athenian democrat whose arrest was ordered to implicate others in the regime's crimes. -- The Tholos (rotunda): The circular building in the Agora where the Prytaneis (executive committee of the Council) dined and conducted business. -- The tribe Antiochis: One of the ten Athenian tribes, which rotated the presidency of the Council. -- Cross-reference: The Gorgias (473e-474a) and Republic (496c-e) reinforce the theme that the just person is endangered in politics. Xenophon (Hellenica 1.7.15) corroborates the Arginusae account. The Seventh Letter (324d-325a), if genuine, describes Plato's own disillusionment with the Thirty.

narrative: Now do you really imagine that I could have survived all these years, if I had led a public life, supposing that like a good man I had always maintained the right and had made justice, as I ought, the first thing? No indeed, men of Athens, neither I nor any other man. But I have been always the same in all my actions, public as well as private, and never have I yielded any base compliance to those who are slanderously termed my disciples, or to any other. Not that I have any regular disciples. But if any one likes to come and hear me while I am pursuing my mission, whether he be young or old, he is not excluded. Nor do I converse only with those who pay; but any one, whether he be rich or poor, may ask and answer me and listen to my words; and whether he turns out to be a bad man or a good one, neither result can be justly imputed to me; for I never taught or professed to teach him anything. And if any one says that he has ever learned or heard anything from me in private which all the world has not heard, let me tell you that he is lying.
refashioned:
Do you really imagine I could have survived all these years if I had led a public life, always standing up for what's right and making justice my first priority? Of course not, men of Athens -- neither I nor anyone else could have.

But I have been consistent in everything I've done, public and private alike. I have never made any base concession to those who are slanderously called my students, or to anyone else. I don't actually have students in the formal sense. But if anyone -- young or old -- wants to come and listen while I pursue my mission, they are welcome. I don't only talk with people who pay; anyone, rich or poor, may question me, answer me, and listen to what I say. Whether any of them turns out well or badly cannot fairly be blamed on me, because I have never taught or claimed to teach anyone anything. And if anyone says they ever heard or learned something from me in private that the rest of the world didn't hear, I can tell you they are lying.
elegant: Socrates could not have survived in public life while insisting on justice. He has been consistent in public and private. He has no formal students -- anyone may listen. He charges no fees. He never taught anyone privately. Whatever his associates become cannot be blamed on him.
summary: Socrates argues he could not have survived as a politician while insisting on justice. He emphasizes he has no formal students, charges no fees, converses openly with anyone, and has never given private teachings. He cannot be held responsible for how his associates turn out.
distilled: Socrates has no formal students, charges nothing, teaches nothing privately, and cannot be blamed for what his listeners become.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- The denial of being a teacher is crucial to Socrates' defense. The Sophists charged fees and claimed to teach virtue; Socrates distinguishes himself from them by denying he teaches anything at all -- he merely inquires. The corruption charge depends on a teacher-student relationship Socrates rejects. -- 'Slanderously termed my disciples' -- likely references Critias (one of the Thirty Tyrants) and Alcibiades (who defected to Sparta), whose association with Socrates was politically damaging despite Socrates' disavowal. -- Cross-reference: The Meno (80d) presents the paradox that Socrates claims to teach nothing. In the Theaetetus (150c-d), Socrates compares himself to a midwife who helps others bring forth their own ideas. Xenophon's Memorabilia (1.2.12-48) directly addresses the Critias and Alcibiades problem.

narrative: But I shall be asked, "Why do people delight in continually conversing with you?" I have told you already, Athenians, the whole truth about this matter: they like to hear the cross-examination of the pretenders to wisdom; there is amusement in it. Now this duty of cross-examining other men has been imposed upon me by God; and has been signified to me by oracles, visions, and in every way in which the will of divine power was ever intimated to any one. This is true, O Athenians, or, if not true, would be soon refuted. If I am or have been corrupting the youth, those of them who are now grown up and have become sensible that I gave them bad advice in the days of their youth should come forward as accusers, and take their revenge; or if they do not like to come themselves, some of their relatives, fathers, brothers, or other kinsmen, should say what evil their families have suffered at my hands.
refashioned:
But I'll be asked: "Why do people enjoy spending so much time talking with you?" I've already told you the whole truth about this, Athenians: they enjoy watching pretenders to wisdom get cross-examined. There's entertainment in it.

This duty of examining others has been imposed on me by God, communicated through oracles, visions, and every way divine will has ever been conveyed to anyone. This is the truth, Athenians -- and if it weren't, it would be easy to disprove.

If I am or have been corrupting the youth, then those young people who are now grown up and realize I gave them harmful advice should step forward as my accusers and take their revenge. And if they don't want to come themselves, their relatives -- fathers, brothers, other family members -- should testify about the harm their families suffered at my hands.
elegant: People enjoy watching Socrates cross-examine pretenders -- that's why they follow him. This mission comes from God through oracles and visions. If he has corrupted anyone, those grown-up former youths or their families should come forward to testify. Where are they?
summary: Socrates explains that people follow him because they enjoy watching pretenders to wisdom be exposed. He reiterates his divine mission and challenges anyone supposedly corrupted by him -- or their families -- to come forward as accusers.
distilled: If Socrates has corrupted anyone, the supposed victims or their families should come forward to testify -- but none do.
notes: Speaker: Socrates, with a hypothetical question. -- The challenge to produce victims is rhetorically powerful and sets up the character witness passage (1.40). The absence of any former associate testifying against Socrates is presented as conclusive evidence against the corruption charge. -- Cross-reference: The divine mission through 'oracles, visions, and every way' expands on the Delphic oracle story (1.7-1.11) and the daimonion (1.36). In the Meno (99c-100a), Socrates discusses divine inspiration as the source of virtue in statesmen.

narrative: Now is their time. Many of them I see in the court. There is Crito, who is of the same age and of the same deme with myself, and there is Critobulus his son, whom I also see. Then again there is Lysanias of Sphettus, who is the father of Aeschines -- he is present; and also there is Antiphon of Cephisus, who is the father of Epigenes; and there are the brothers of several who have associated with me. There is Nicostratus the son of Theosdotides, and the brother of Theodotus (now Theodotus himself is dead, and therefore he, at any rate, will not seek to stop him); and there is Paralus the son of Demodocus, who had a brother Theages; and Adeimantus the son of Ariston, whose brother Plato is present; and Aeantodorus, who is the brother of Apollodorus, whom I also see. I might mention a great many others, some of whom Meletus should have produced as witnesses in the course of his speech; and let him still produce them, if he has forgotten -- I will make way for him. And let him say, if he has any testimony of the sort which he can produce. Nay, Athenians, the very opposite is the truth. For all these are ready to witness on behalf of the corrupter, of the injurer of their kindred, as Meletus and Anytus call me; not the corrupted youth only -- there might have been a motive for that -- but their uncorrupted elder relatives. Why should they too support me with their testimony? Why, indeed, except for the sake of truth and justice, and because they know that I am speaking the truth, and that Meletus is a liar.
refashioned:
Now is the time for them to come forward. I see many of them in this courtroom. There is Crito, my own age and from my own deme, and his son Critobulus, whom I also see. There is Lysanias of Sphettus, father of Aeschines -- he is present. There is Antiphon of Cephisus, father of Epigenes. And there are brothers of several who have spent time with me: Nicostratus, son of Theozotides and brother of Theodotus (Theodotus himself is dead, so at least he can't prevent his brother from testifying); Paralus, son of Demodocus, whose brother was Theages; Adeimantus, son of Ariston, whose brother Plato is here in court; and Aeantodorus, brother of Apollodorus, whom I also see.

I could name many more. Meletus should have called some of them as witnesses during his speech. If he forgot, let him do it now -- I'll yield my time. Let him produce any such testimony if he can.

But the truth, Athenians, is exactly the opposite. All these people are ready to testify on behalf of the supposed corrupter, the supposed injurer of their family members -- as Meletus and Anytus call me. Not just the young associates themselves (who might have self-interested motives), but their uncorrupted older relatives too. Why would they support me? For no reason except truth and justice, because they know I am telling the truth and Meletus is lying.
elegant: The courtroom is full of Socrates' associates and their families -- Crito, Critobulus, Plato, Apollodorus, and many others. Not one testifies against him. Meletus produced none as witnesses. Instead, they are all ready to testify for the alleged corrupter. The older relatives especially have no motive except truth.
summary: Socrates names numerous associates and their family members present in court -- including Crito, Plato, and Apollodorus. None accuse him; all are prepared to testify in his defense. He challenges Meletus to produce any of them as hostile witnesses. Their support, especially from the older relatives, can only be motivated by truth.
distilled: The families of Socrates' associates fill the courtroom ready to defend him -- not one comes forward to accuse, proving Meletus's corruption charge is a lie.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- This passage provides a snapshot of Socrates' circle. Key figures: Crito (wealthy Athenian, title character of the Crito, who will later try to arrange Socrates' escape); Critobulus (Crito's son, appears in Xenophon); Aeschines of Sphettus (Socratic philosopher and dialogue writer, not the later orator); Epigenes (mentioned in Phaedo 59b as present at Socrates' death); Plato (here mentioned by name as brother of Adeimantus -- the same Adeimantus who is an interlocutor in the Republic); Apollodorus (passionate follower, narrator of the Symposium, present at the death in Phaedo 59a, described as weeping uncontrollably). -- The deme (local district) shared by Socrates and Crito is Alopece. -- Cross-reference: Many of these figures appear in the Phaedo's list of those present at Socrates' death (59b-c). Adeimantus and his brother Glaucon (not mentioned here) are the main interlocutors of the Republic.

narrative: Well, Athenians, this and the like of this is all the defence which I have to offer. Yet a word more. Perhaps there may be some one who is offended at me, when he calls to mind how he himself on a similar, or even a less serious occasion, prayed and entreated the judges with many tears, and how he produced his children in court, which was a moving spectacle, together with a host of relations and friends; whereas I, who am probably in danger of my life, will do none of these things. The contrast may occur to his mind, and he may be set against me, and vote in anger because he is displeased at me on this account.
refashioned: Well, Athenians, this -- and others like it -- is the whole defense I have to offer. But one more word. Perhaps someone among you is irritated with me, remembering how he himself, on a similar or even less serious occasion, begged and pleaded with the jury through his tears, parading his children before the court as a moving spectacle, along with a crowd of relatives and friends. And here I am, probably facing death, and I will do none of this. The contrast may come to his mind, and it may turn him against me -- he may vote in anger simply because he is annoyed by my refusal.
elegant: Some jurors may resent Socrates for refusing to beg, weep, or parade his children before the court as others do. The contrast with their own courtroom behavior may anger them into voting against him.
summary: Socrates acknowledges that some jurors may be offended by his refusal to follow the convention of weeping, begging, and displaying his children to win sympathy, especially when they themselves did so in their own trials.
distilled: Some jurors may vote against Socrates out of resentment that he refuses to beg, weep, or parade his family as they did in their own trials.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- Emotional appeals were standard courtroom practice in Athenian trials. Defendants routinely brought weeping children and relatives before the jury to arouse pity. Socrates' refusal to do this was culturally provocative and likely cost him votes. -- Cross-reference: In the Gorgias (521e-522a), Socrates predicts he would be convicted because he would not flatter the jury. The Phaedo (116a-117a) shows Socrates maintaining the same composure at his death, rebuking those who weep. Aristotle's Rhetoric (1.1, 1354a) later criticizes emotional appeals as irrelevant to justice.

narrative: Now if there be such a person among you, -- mind, I do not say that there is, -- to him I may fairly reply: "My friend, I am a man, and like other men, a creature of flesh and blood, and not 'of wood or stone,' as Homer says; and I have a family, yes, and sons, O Athenians, three in number, one almost a man, and two others who are still young; and yet I will not bring any of them hither in order to petition you for an acquittal." And why not? Not from any self-assertion or want of respect for you. Whether I am or am not afraid of death is another question, of which I will not now speak. But, having regard to public opinion, I feel that such conduct would be discreditable to myself, and to you, and to the whole state. One who has reached my years, and who has a name for wisdom, ought not to demean himself. Whether this opinion of me be deserved or not, at any rate the world has decided that Socrates is in some way superior to other men. And if those among you who are said to be superior in wisdom and courage, and any other virtue, demean themselves in this way, how shameful is their conduct! I have seen men of reputation, when they have been condemned, behaving in the strangest manner: they seemed to fancy that they were going to suffer something dreadful if they died, and that they could be immortal if you only allowed them to live; and I think that such are a dishonour to the state, and that any stranger coming in would have said of them that the most eminent men of Athens, to whom the Athenians themselves give honour and command, are no better than women. And I say that these things ought not to be done by those of us who have a reputation; and if they are done, you ought not to permit them; you ought rather to show that you are far more disposed to condemn the man who gets up a doleful scene and makes the city ridiculous, than him who holds his peace.
refashioned:
Now, if such a person exists among you -- and I'm not saying there is -- I might fairly reply: "My friend, I am a man, flesh and blood like everyone else, not made 'of wood or stone' as Homer puts it. I have a family -- yes, three sons, one nearly grown and two still young. And yet I will not bring them here to beg for my acquittal."

Why not? Not out of arrogance or disrespect for you. Whether I fear death is a separate question I won't address now. But I believe such behavior would be discreditable to me, to you, and to the entire city. A man of my age, with whatever reputation for wisdom I may have, should not degrade himself this way. Whether I deserve that reputation or not, the world has decided that Socrates is in some way set apart. If men who are supposed to be superior in wisdom, courage, and other virtues debase themselves by groveling, how shameful that is!

I've seen prominent men behave in the most bizarre ways after being condemned -- as if dying were some unimaginable horror, and as if you could make them immortal by letting them live. Such men are a disgrace to the city. Any foreigner visiting would conclude that Athens's most distinguished leaders -- the men you yourselves honor and elect -- are no better than women.

I say that those of us with any reputation should not behave this way. And if we do, you should not permit it. You should be far more inclined to condemn the man who stages a pathetic spectacle and embarrasses the city than the man who keeps his composure.
elegant: Socrates has three sons but will not parade them for pity. A man of his age and reputation should not grovel. He has seen condemned men behave as if death were uniquely terrible and the jury could grant immortality -- disgracing themselves and Athens. The jury should condemn theatrical weeping, not dignified silence.
summary: Socrates reveals he has three sons but refuses to bring them to court for sympathy. He argues that groveling is beneath a person of reputation and disgraces the city. He has watched condemned men behave absurdly, as though the jury could grant immortality. The jury should punish theatrical spectacles, not dignified composure.
distilled: Despite having three sons, Socrates refuses to parade them for pity because groveling degrades himself, the jury, and Athens itself.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- 'Of wood or stone' is from Homer's Odyssey (19.163), where Penelope says she is not made of oak or stone. Socrates' three sons: Lamprocles (nearly grown), Sophroniscus, and Menexenus (still young). -- The remark that groveling men 'are no better than women' reflects conventional Greek gender attitudes. -- Cross-reference: Socrates' sons appear at the end of the Phaedo (116b), when Socrates says goodbye to them before drinking the hemlock. The Crito (45c-d) has Crito urging Socrates to think of his sons, suggesting the family obligation was a live concern. Xanthippe (Socrates' wife) appears briefly in the Phaedo (60a), weeping and being sent away.

narrative: But, setting aside the question of public opinion, there seems to be something wrong in asking a favour of a judge, and thus procuring an acquittal, instead of informing and convincing him. For his duty is, not to make a present of justice, but to give judgment; and he has sworn that he will judge according to the laws, and not according to his own good pleasure; and we ought not to encourage you, nor should you allow yourselves to be encouraged, in this habit of perjury -- there can be no piety in that. Do not then require me to do what I consider dishonourable and impious and wrong, especially now, when I am being tried for impiety on the indictment of Meletus. For if, O men of Athens, by force of persuasion and entreaty I could overpower your oaths, then I should be teaching you to believe that there are no gods, and in defending should simply convict myself of the charge of not believing in them. But that is not so -- far otherwise. For I do believe that there are gods, and in a sense higher than that in which any of my accusers believe in them. And to you and to God I commit my cause, to be determined by you as is best for you and me.
refashioned:
But beyond public opinion, there is something fundamentally wrong with asking a judge for a favor to obtain an acquittal, rather than informing and convincing him. A judge's duty is not to hand out justice as a gift but to render judgment. He has sworn to judge according to the laws, not according to his personal sympathies. We should not encourage you to break that oath, and you should not allow yourselves to be encouraged in that direction -- there can be no piety in perjury.

So do not ask me to do what I consider dishonorable, impious, and wrong -- especially now, when I am being tried for impiety on Meletus's indictment. If I could overpower your oaths through persuasion and pleading, I would in fact be teaching you that there are no gods. In defending myself, I would be proving the charge against me.

But the reality is quite the opposite. I do believe in the gods, and in a deeper sense than any of my accusers do. And so I commit my cause to you and to God, to be decided as is best for both you and me.
elegant: Begging a judge perverts justice into a favor and encourages perjury. If Socrates could override the jury's oaths through emotional appeal, he would be proving the atheism charge -- teaching them the gods don't matter. He believes in the gods more deeply than his accusers do. He commits his cause to the jury and to God.
summary: Socrates makes a final argument against emotional appeals: they corrupt justice by turning judgment into a favor and encourage jury perjury. If he could override their oaths through pleading, he would be proving the atheism charge against himself. He declares his belief in the gods is deeper than his accusers' and commits his fate to the jury and to God.
distilled: Begging the jury would corrupt justice and prove the impiety charge -- so Socrates commits his cause to their judgment and to God.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- This concludes the first speech (Part 1) of the Apology. The argument is elegant: emotional appeals subvert the jury's oath, which is sworn before the gods; therefore, making such appeals is itself a form of impiety. Socrates turns the impiety charge against the prosecution's own methods. -- The juror's oath (the Heliastic oath) required judging according to the laws of Athens. -- Cross-reference: The relationship between piety and justice is the central question of the Euthyphro. The commitment of one's cause to God echoes the trust in divine providence expressed throughout the Apology and reaffirmed at 3.4 and 3.7. The Crito (54b-c) ends similarly with Socrates accepting the divine will.

narrative: There are many reasons why I am not grieved, O men of Athens, at the vote of condemnation. I expected it, and am only surprised that the votes are so nearly equal; for I had thought that the majority against me would have been far larger; but now, had thirty votes gone over to the other side, I should have been acquitted. And I may say, I think, that I have escaped Meletus. I may say more; for without the assistance of Anytus and Lycon, any one may see that he would not have had a fifth part of the votes, as the law requires, in which case he would have incurred a fine of a thousand drachmae.
refashioned:
There are many reasons I am not distressed, men of Athens, by the vote of condemnation. I expected it. In fact, I'm only surprised the margin was so close -- I assumed the majority against me would be far larger. As it stands, if just thirty votes had shifted to the other side, I would have been acquitted.

And I believe I can fairly say that I have beaten Meletus. Indeed, I can say more: without the backing of Anytus and Lycon, anyone can see that Meletus would not have received even a fifth of the votes, as the law requires. In that case, he would have been fined a thousand drachmas.
elegant: Socrates is unsurprised by conviction but surprised by how close the vote was -- thirty votes would have acquitted him. Without Anytus and Lycon's support, Meletus alone would have failed to reach the required one-fifth threshold and been fined a thousand drachmas.
summary: After his conviction, Socrates expresses no grief and notes the surprisingly close vote. A shift of just thirty votes would have acquitted him. He claims victory over Meletus specifically, noting that without Anytus and Lycon's support, Meletus would have failed to meet the minimum vote threshold.
distilled: Socrates is unsurprised by conviction but notes the close margin -- thirty votes from acquittal -- and claims that Meletus alone would have failed entirely.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- This begins Part 2 of the Apology, the penalty phase (timesis). Athenian law allowed the defendant to propose a counter-penalty after conviction. -- The vote count: with a jury of 501, a shift of 30 votes means approximately 280-221 or similar. Athenian law required the prosecutor to receive at least one-fifth of the votes or face a 1,000 drachma fine for frivolous prosecution. -- Anytus was a wealthy tanner and democratic politician who was likely the true driving force behind the prosecution. Lycon was a rhetorician. -- Cross-reference: Diogenes Laertius (2.41-42) records the vote counts, though the exact numbers vary in ancient sources.

narrative: And so he proposes death as the penalty. And what shall I propose on my part, O men of Athens? Clearly that which is my due. And what is my due? What return shall be made to the man who has never had the wit to be idle during his whole life; but has been careless of what the many care for -- wealth, and family interests, and military offices, and speaking in the assembly, and magistracies, and plots, and parties. Reflecting that I was really too honest a man to be a politician and live, I did not go where I could do no good to you or to myself; but where I could do the greatest good privately to every one of you, thither I went, and sought to persuade every man among you that he must look to himself, and seek virtue and wisdom before he looks to his private interests, and look to the state before he looks to the interests of the state; and that this should be the order which he observes in all his actions. What shall be done to such an one? Doubtless some good thing, O men of Athens, if he has his reward; and the good should be of a kind suitable to him. What would be a reward suitable to a poor man who is your benefactor, and who desires leisure that he may instruct you? There can be no reward so fitting as maintenance in the Prytaneum, O men of Athens, a reward which he deserves far more than the citizen who has won the prize at Olympia in the horse or chariot race, whether the chariots were drawn by two horses or by many. For I am in want, and he has enough; and he only gives you the appearance of happiness, and I give you the reality. And if I am to estimate the penalty fairly, I should say that maintenance in the Prytaneum is the just return.
refashioned:
So Meletus proposes death. And what do I propose, men of Athens? Obviously, what I deserve. And what do I deserve? What reward is owed to a man who has never had the sense to sit idle, who has neglected everything most people care about -- wealth, family advancement, military commands, political speeches, magistracies, conspiracies, and factions? Recognizing that I was too honest to enter politics and survive, I avoided the path where I could do no good to you or myself. Instead, I went where I could do the greatest good -- approaching each of you privately, trying to persuade every person to examine themselves, to seek virtue and wisdom before attending to private interests, and to care for the city's character before its material interests. This should be the order governing all our actions.

What should be done for such a person? Surely something good, men of Athens, if he's to receive what he actually deserves -- and the reward should suit his situation. What reward fits a poor man who serves as your benefactor and needs the leisure to continue instructing you?

There is no more fitting reward than free meals in the Prytaneum, men of Athens. And I deserve this far more than the citizen who wins the horse or chariot race at Olympia, whether with a two-horse or four-horse team. He has plenty and I have nothing. He gives you only the appearance of happiness; I give you the reality. If I'm to assess my penalty fairly, I say: maintenance in the Prytaneum is the just return.
elegant: Meletus proposes death. Socrates proposes what he deserves: free meals in the Prytaneum, the honor given to Olympic victors. He neglected wealth, career, and politics to serve Athens as its benefactor. Olympic champions give the appearance of happiness; Socrates gives the reality. He is poor and needs the leisure to continue his work.
summary: In response to the death penalty, Socrates provocatively proposes that he deserves free maintenance in the Prytaneum -- the honor reserved for Olympic victors and distinguished citizens. He argues he has sacrificed everything to benefit Athens privately and deserves this more than athletes, who provide only the appearance of happiness.
distilled: Rather than a penalty, Socrates proposes he deserves the reward of free meals in the Prytaneum -- more than Olympic victors, since he gives Athens real happiness, not mere spectacle.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- The Prytaneum (city hall) provided free meals at public expense to Olympic victors, distinguished citizens, and their descendants -- one of Athens' highest honors. Socrates' proposal was audaciously provocative and likely alienated jurors who might have supported a moderate penalty. -- The distinction between appearance and reality of happiness echoes Socratic philosophy's emphasis on true versus conventional goods. -- Cross-reference: The Gorgias (464b-465e) distinguishes between flattery (which gives the appearance of good) and genuine arts (which produce real good). The Republic (Book IX, 576b-588a) argues the philosopher has true happiness while the tyrant only appears happy. The counter-penalty process (timesis) gave the jury a choice between the prosecution's and the defendant's proposed penalties.

narrative: Perhaps you think that I am braving you in what I am saying now, as in what I said before about the tears and prayers. But this is not so. I speak rather because I am convinced that I never intentionally wronged any one, although I cannot convince you -- the time has been too short; if there were a law at Athens, as there is in other cities, that a capital cause should not be decided in one day, then I believe that I should have convinced you. But I cannot in a moment refute great slanders; and, as I am convinced that I never wronged another, I will assuredly not wrong myself. I will not say of myself that I deserve any evil, or propose any penalty. Why should I? because I am afraid of the penalty of death which Meletus proposes? When I do not know whether death is a good or an evil, why should I propose a penalty which would certainly be an evil? Shall I say imprisonment? And why should I live in prison, and be the slave of the magistrates of the year -- of the Eleven? Or shall the penalty be a fine, and imprisonment until the fine is paid? There is the same objection. I should have to lie in prison, for money I have none, and cannot pay. And if I say exile (and this may possibly be the penalty which you will affix), I must indeed be blinded by the love of life, if I am so irrational as to expect that when you, who are my own citizens, cannot endure my discourses and words, and have found them so grievous and odious that you will have no more of them, others are likely to endure me. No indeed, men of Athens, that is not very likely. And what a life should I lead, at my age, wandering from city to city, ever changing my place of exile, and always being driven out! For I am quite sure that wherever I go, there, as here, the young men will flock to me; and if I drive them away, their elders will drive me out at their request; and if I let them come, their fathers and friends will drive me out for their sakes.
refashioned:
Perhaps you think I'm being defiant now, as I was when I spoke about the tears and prayers. But I'm not. I speak this way because I am genuinely convinced I never intentionally wronged anyone -- though I can't convince you of that. The time has been too short. If Athens had a law like other cities, requiring that capital cases take more than one day to decide, I believe I could have convinced you. But I cannot refute years of slander in a few hours.

Since I am certain I never wronged anyone else, I certainly won't wrong myself. I won't say I deserve punishment or propose one. Why should I? Because I fear the death penalty Meletus proposes? I don't even know whether death is good or evil -- so why would I choose a penalty I know is evil?

Shall I propose imprisonment? Why should I live in a cell, enslaved to whatever magistrates hold office that year -- the Eleven? Shall I propose a fine with imprisonment until it's paid? Same problem: I have no money and would rot in prison. Shall I propose exile? If I were that desperate to cling to life, I'd have to be irrational. If you, my own fellow citizens, can't tolerate my conversations and find them so unbearable you want to be rid of me, why would I expect strangers to put up with me?

That's not very likely, men of Athens. And what kind of life would it be -- at my age, wandering from city to city, constantly expelled? I'm quite sure that wherever I go, young people will gather around me just as they do here. If I drive them away, their elders will banish me at their request. If I welcome them, their fathers and friends will banish me for their sakes.
elegant: Socrates refuses to wrong himself by proposing a punishment he doesn't deserve. Death may be good or evil -- he doesn't know. Imprisonment means slavery to magistrates. He has no money for fines. Exile is irrational: if Athens can't tolerate him, no city will. Wherever he goes, young people will follow, and he'll be expelled again.
summary: Socrates systematically rejects every conventional penalty. He won't propose punishment because he's done nothing wrong. Death is unknowable; imprisonment is slavery; he has no money for fines; exile is pointless since no city will tolerate him any better than Athens does, and he'd spend old age wandering.
