distilled: Socrates rejects imprisonment, fines, and exile as penalties -- each is either certainly evil or pointless, while death remains unknowable.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- The Eleven were the magistrates who oversaw the prison and executions in Athens. -- The reference to 'other cities' where capital cases take more than one day likely refers to Sparta or other Greek poleis. Athenian trials were notoriously compressed -- typically decided in a single day. -- Socrates' refusal to propose a serious counter-penalty was likely decisive in the final sentencing vote. Ancient sources suggest the margin increased significantly at the penalty phase. -- Cross-reference: The question of whether death is good or evil recurs throughout: see 1.31 and 3.5. The Crito (52e-53a) addresses exile, with the Laws of Athens arguing that Socrates' voluntary lifetime residency implies acceptance of their authority. The Phaedo (62b-c) discusses whether one may take one's own life.

narrative: Some one will say: "Yes, Socrates, but cannot you hold your tongue, and then you may go into a foreign city, and no one will interfere with you?" Now I have great difficulty in making you understand my answer to this. For if I tell you that to do as you say would be a disobedience to the God, and therefore that I cannot hold my tongue, you will not believe that I am serious; and if I say again that daily to discourse about virtue, and of those other things about which you hear me examining myself and others, is the greatest good of man, and that the unexamined life is not worth living, you are still less likely to believe me. Yet I say what is true, although a thing of which it is hard for me to persuade you. Also, I have never been accustomed to think that I deserve to suffer any harm. Had I money I might have estimated the offence at what I was able to pay, and not have been much the worse. But I have none, and therefore I must ask you to proportion the fine to my means. Well, perhaps I could afford a mina, and therefore I propose that penalty: Plato, Crito, Critobulus, and Apollodorus, my friends here, bid me say thirty minae, and they will be the sureties. Let thirty minae be the penalty; for which sum they will be ample security to you.
refashioned:
Someone will say: "But Socrates, can't you just keep quiet? Then you could go to a foreign city and no one would bother you." I have great difficulty making you understand my answer to this.

If I tell you that keeping quiet would mean disobeying God, and that therefore I cannot do it, you won't believe I'm serious. And if I tell you that discussing virtue every day -- examining myself and others on the subjects you've heard me pursue -- is the greatest good a human being can do, and that the unexamined life is not worth living, you're even less likely to believe me.

Yet what I say is true, even though it is hard to persuade you of it.

Besides, I have never thought I deserve to suffer any harm. If I had money, I could have proposed a fine I could actually afford, and it wouldn't have been much of a hardship. But I have none, so I must ask you to set the fine in proportion to my means. I could perhaps afford one mina, so I propose that as my penalty.

But Plato, Crito, Critobulus, and Apollodorus -- my friends here -- urge me to propose thirty minae, and they will guarantee payment. So let thirty minae be the penalty. They will be ample security for you.
elegant: Can't Socrates just stay quiet? No -- silence would disobey God, and the unexamined life is not worth living. He knows the jury won't believe either reason, but both are true. Having no money, he proposes one mina. His friends -- Plato, Crito, Critobulus, Apollodorus -- guarantee thirty minae instead.
summary: Socrates addresses the suggestion that he simply stop philosophizing: silence would disobey God, and the unexamined life is not worth living. He proposes a fine of one mina (all he can afford), but his friends Plato, Crito, Critobulus, and Apollodorus offer to guarantee thirty minae as his counter-penalty.
distilled: The unexamined life is not worth living and silence would disobey God -- so Socrates proposes a fine of thirty minae, guaranteed by his friends.
notes: Speaker: Socrates. -- 'The unexamined life is not worth living' (ho de anexetastos bios ou biotos anthropo) is perhaps the single most famous line in Western philosophy. -- One mina = 100 drachmas. Thirty minae = 3,000 drachmas, a substantial sum (roughly several years' wages for a laborer). The offer by friends to guarantee the fine was Socrates' actual counter-proposal. -- Plato, Crito, Critobulus, and Apollodorus: four of Socrates' closest associates. This is one of only three places where Plato mentions himself by name in his dialogues (the others being 1.40 and Phaedo 59b). -- Cross-reference: The Crito dramatizes the aftermath: Crito visits Socrates in prison and offers to finance his escape, which Socrates refuses. The Phaedo (59b) lists those present at the death; notably 'Plato, I believe, was ill.' The principle that the unexamined life is not worth living is the foundation of the entire Socratic enterprise as depicted across all the early dialogues.

narrative: Not much time will be gained, O Athenians, in return for the evil name which you will get from the detractors of the city, who will say that you killed Socrates, a wise man; for they will call me wise, even although I am not wise, when they want to reproach you. If you had waited a little while, your desire would have been fulfilled in the course of nature. For I am far advanced in years, as you may perceive, and not far from death. I am speaking now not to all of you, but only to those who have condemned me to death. And I have another thing to say to them: you think that I was convicted because I had no words of the sort which would have procured my acquittal--I mean, if I had thought fit to leave nothing undone or unsaid. Not so; the deficiency which led to my conviction was not of words--certainly not. But I had not the boldness or impudence or inclination to address you as you would have liked me to do, weeping and wailing and lamenting, and saying and doing many things which you have been accustomed to hear from others, and which, as I maintain, are unworthy of me. I thought at the time that I ought not to do anything common or mean when in danger: nor do I now repent of the style of my defence; I would rather die having spoken after my manner, than speak in your manner and live.
refashioned:
You have not gained much time, Athenians, and in exchange you will bear the shame of being called the men who killed Socrates, a wise man. Your critics will call me wise whether I am or not, because they want to wound you. If you had simply waited a little while, nature would have given you what you wanted. I am old, as you can see, and already near death.

I speak now only to those who voted to condemn me. You think I was convicted because I lacked the right words -- that if I had been willing to say and do anything, I could have been acquitted. That is not what happened. It was not words I lacked. What I lacked was the shamelessness to address you the way you wanted -- weeping, wailing, begging, performing the kind of spectacle you are used to hearing from other men and which I consider beneath me. I believed then, and I believe now, that a man in danger should not resort to anything base or degrading. I do not regret how I spoke. I would rather die having spoken truthfully in my own way than survive by speaking in yours.
elegant:
You gain little by this verdict, Athenians, and you will bear the reproach of having killed a man the world calls wise. Had you waited, nature would have done the work for you -- I am old and near death already.

To those who condemned me: you think I lost for lack of words. No. I lost because I refused to weep and beg the way you expected. I would rather die having spoken honestly than live having debased myself to win your favor.
summary: Socrates addresses those who condemned him, noting they gain little since he would have died naturally soon. He insists his conviction came not from lack of eloquence but from his refusal to debase himself with weeping and begging, and he would rather die with dignity than live without it.
distilled: Socrates declares he would rather die having spoken with integrity than survive by abandoning it.
notes: This passage opens Socrates' final address after the death sentence has been pronounced. His claim that he would rather die speaking truthfully than live by debasing himself is one of the Apology's most quoted formulations and establishes the template for philosophical martyrdom. The distinction between losing through lack of words versus losing through refusal to perform emotional theater anticipates the Stoic distinction between things within our control (our character, our speech) and things outside it (the verdict). Compare Crito 46b-c, where Socrates reaffirms that he has always followed reason rather than popular pressure. The statement 'I would rather die having spoken after my manner, than speak in your manner and live' became a touchstone for later thinkers on integrity under coercion -- from Epictetus to Thomas More to Thoreau. Socrates' observation that the Athenians need only have waited for nature to take its course adds pointed irony: they chose to stain their reputation for something time would have given them freely.

narrative: For neither in war nor yet at law ought I or any man to use every way of escaping death. Often in battle there can be no doubt that if a man will throw away his arms, and fall on his knees before his pursuers, he may escape death; and in other dangers there are other ways of escaping death, if a man is willing to say and do anything. The difficulty, my friends, is not to avoid death, but to avoid unrighteousness; for that runs faster than death. I am old and move slowly, and the slower runner has overtaken me, and my accusers are keen and quick, and the faster runner, who is unrighteousness, has overtaken them. And now I depart hence condemned by you to suffer the penalty of death,--they too go their ways condemned by the truth to suffer the penalty of villainy and wrong; and I must abide by my award--let them abide by theirs. I suppose that these things may be regarded as fated,--and I think that they are well.
refashioned:
Neither in war nor in a court of law should any man resort to every possible means of escaping death. In battle, a soldier can always throw down his weapons and beg on his knees -- he may survive. In other dangers there are other compromises, if a man is willing to say and do anything at all. But the real difficulty, my friends, is not escaping death. The real difficulty is escaping unrighteousness -- because unrighteousness runs faster than death.

I am old and slow, and the slower runner -- death -- has caught me. My accusers are sharp and quick, and the faster runner -- wickedness -- has caught them. Now I leave this court condemned by you to die. They leave condemned by truth itself to bear the penalty of their dishonesty and injustice. I must accept my sentence. Let them accept theirs. Perhaps all of this was fated. And I think it is well.
elegant:
Neither in battle nor at trial should a man do anything to escape death. A soldier can always throw down his weapons and beg. But the real challenge is not outrunning death -- it is outrunning wickedness, which is faster.

Death, the slower runner, has caught me. Unrighteousness, the faster runner, has caught my accusers. I leave condemned to die; they leave condemned by truth to bear the weight of injustice. Let each of us accept our sentence.
summary: Socrates draws an analogy between battlefield cowardice and courtroom dishonesty, arguing the real danger is not death but unrighteousness. Death has caught him, but injustice has caught his accusers -- each party departs with its own sentence.
distilled: The real race is not against death but against wickedness, and wickedness runs faster.
notes: The image of death and unrighteousness as two runners -- one slow, one fast -- is among the most powerful metaphors in the Apology. Socrates inverts the apparent outcome of the trial: he is the one who escapes the worse fate, while his accusers are caught by the swifter pursuer. This inversion of victim and victor prefigures a central Stoic principle -- that moral harm is the only real harm, and that injustice damages the perpetrator more than the victim. Compare Republic 335d, where Socrates argues that harming others only makes them worse and thus harms the harmer. The battlefield analogy connects to his earlier testimony about Potidaea, Amphipolis, and Delium (1.37), completing the parallel between military and philosophical courage. The closing acceptance -- 'I think that they are well' -- anticipates the serene acceptance of death in the Phaedo. Marcus Aurelius and Epictetus both echo this framework: what matters is not what happens to you, but whether you acted justly.

narrative: And now, O men who have condemned me, I would fain prophesy to you; for I am about to die, and in the hour of death men are gifted with prophetic power. And I prophesy to you who are my murderers, that immediately after my departure punishment far heavier than you have inflicted on me will surely await you. Me you have killed because you wanted to escape the accuser, and not to give an account of your lives. But that will not be as you suppose: far otherwise. For I say that there will be more accusers of you than there are now; accusers whom hitherto I have restrained: and as they are younger they will be more inconsiderate with you, and you will be more offended at them. If you think that by killing men you can prevent some one from censuring your evil lives, you are mistaken; that is not a way of escape which is either possible or honourable; the easiest and the noblest way is not to be disabling others, but to be improving yourselves. This is the prophecy which I utter before my departure to the judges who have condemned me.
refashioned:
And now, to those who condemned me, I will make a prophecy -- for I am about to die, and men on the threshold of death are granted prophetic power.

I prophesy this to you, my executioners: immediately after I am gone, a punishment far heavier than what you have dealt me will fall upon you. You killed me because you wanted to escape being questioned -- because you did not want to give an account of your lives. But it will not work out the way you imagine. Not at all. There will be more accusers after me than there are now -- accusers I have been holding back. They are younger than I am, and they will be harsher with you, and you will resent them even more.

If you think you can silence criticism by killing the critic, you are deeply mistaken. That escape is neither possible nor honorable. The easiest and noblest path is not to suppress others but to improve yourselves.

This is the prophecy I leave with those who condemned me.
elegant: I prophesy to you who condemned me: after I am gone, far heavier punishment awaits you. You killed me to escape being questioned. But more accusers will come -- younger and harsher than I was. Killing the critic does not silence the criticism. The only real escape is not to suppress others but to become better yourselves.
summary: Socrates prophesies that killing him will not stop the questioning but multiply it, as younger and more aggressive followers will take his place. The only honorable escape from criticism is self-improvement, not silencing critics.
distilled: Killing the questioner does not kill the questions -- it multiplies them.
notes: This is Socrates' prophecy to his condemners, and history proved it remarkably accurate. The execution of Socrates did not end philosophical inquiry in Athens but catalyzed it: Plato, Xenophon, Antisthenes, Aristippus, Euclides, and others all founded schools or wrote extensively in response to his death. The claim that 'in the hour of death men are gifted with prophetic power' draws on a widespread Greek belief (compare the dying prophecies in Homer and tragedy). Socrates' warning that silencing a critic only produces more critics became foundational to arguments for free speech and open inquiry. The phrase 'accusers whom hitherto I have restrained' suggests Socrates saw himself as moderating rather than inciting his followers -- a direct counter to the corruption charge. This passage influenced the Christian martyrdom tradition, where the blood of martyrs was said to be 'the seed of the church' (Tertullian). Compare also the Crito, where Socrates distinguishes between the opinion of the many and the opinion of the one who understands -- here he extends that distinction: the many cannot escape truth by eliminating the one who speaks it.

narrative: Friends, who would have acquitted me, I would like also to talk with you about the thing which has come to pass, while the magistrates are busy, and before I go to the place at which I must die. Stay then a little, for we may as well talk with one another while there is time. You are my friends, and I should like to show you the meaning of this event which has happened to me. O my judges--for you I may truly call judges--I should like to tell you of a wonderful circumstance. Hitherto the divine faculty of which the internal oracle is the source has constantly been in the habit of opposing me even about trifles, if I was going to make a slip or error in any matter; and now as you see there has come upon me that which may be thought, and is generally believed to be, the last and worst evil. But the oracle made no sign of opposition, either when I was leaving my house in the morning, or when I was on my way to the court, or while I was speaking, at anything which I was going to say; and yet I have often been stopped in the middle of a speech, but now in nothing I either said or did touching the matter in hand has the oracle opposed me. What do I take to be the explanation of this silence? I will tell you. It is an intimation that what has happened to me is a good, and that those of us who think that death is an evil are in error. For the customary sign would surely have opposed me had I been going to evil and not to good.
refashioned:
Friends -- those of you who would have acquitted me -- I would like to talk with you about what has happened, while the officials are occupied and before I go to the place where I must die. Stay a moment. We may as well speak with one another while there is still time. You are my friends, and I want to show you the meaning of what has taken place. You, I may truly call judges -- for that is what you are.

Let me tell you something remarkable. All my life, the divine sign -- that inner voice -- has opposed me constantly, even in small matters, whenever I was about to make any kind of mistake. Yet now, when what most people consider the ultimate evil has come upon me, the sign has been completely silent. It did not oppose me when I left my house this morning. It did not oppose me on my way to the court. It did not oppose me at any point during my speech -- though in the past it has often stopped me in the middle of a sentence.

At no point today, in anything I said or did, has the sign opposed me. What does this silence mean? I will tell you. It means that what has happened to me is good. Those of us who believe death is an evil are wrong. The sign would surely have stopped me if I had been heading toward something bad rather than something good.
elegant:
Friends who would have acquitted me -- stay a moment while there is time. I want to show you the meaning of what has happened.

All my life, the divine sign has opposed me whenever I was about to make even a small mistake. Yet today -- when what people call the worst evil has befallen me -- the sign has been completely silent. It did not stop me this morning, on the road, or during my speech. This silence means that what has happened is good. Death is not the evil we suppose. If it were, the sign would have warned me.
summary: Socrates addresses his supporters, noting that his divine sign -- which has always warned him before mistakes, even trivial ones -- remained completely silent throughout the trial. He interprets this silence as proof that his death is not an evil but a good.
distilled: The divine sign's silence throughout the trial reveals that death is not the evil people assume it to be.
notes: The argument from the silence of the daimonion (divine sign) is one of the most distinctive and personal arguments in the Apology. Socrates' inner voice is discussed across multiple dialogues -- in the Phaedrus (242b-c) it stops him mid-speech, and in the Republic (496c) it is linked to his avoidance of politics. Here its silence becomes positive evidence: an argument from absence. This passage is crucial for understanding Socrates' relationship to the divine -- the sign only prohibits, never commands, making its silence at the most consequential moment of his life deeply significant. The address to 'friends who would have acquitted me' shifts the tone dramatically from the stern prophecy of 3.3 to intimate philosophical conversation. Socrates calls only these sympathetic jurors 'true judges,' implying that genuine judgment requires not just legal authority but moral discernment. The reasoning anticipates the more elaborate arguments about death in the Phaedo, where Socrates develops the case that the soul may survive the body. Here the argument is more modest -- based on his personal experience of the sign -- but no less compelling for its simplicity.

narrative: Let us reflect in another way, and we shall see that there is great reason to hope that death is a good; for one of two things--either death is a state of nothingness and utter unconsciousness, or, as men say, there is a change and migration of the soul from this world to another. Now if you suppose that there is no consciousness, but a sleep like the sleep of him who is undisturbed even by dreams, death will be an unspeakable gain. For if a person were to select the night in which his sleep was undisturbed even by dreams, and were to compare with this the other days and nights of his life, and then were to tell us how many days and nights he had passed in the course of his life better and more pleasantly than this one, I think that any man, I will not say a private man, but even the great king will not find many such days or nights, when compared with the others. Now if death be of such a nature, I say that to die is gain; for eternity is then only a single night.
refashioned:
Let us consider the question from another angle, and we will find strong reason to hope that death is a good thing. There are only two possibilities. Either death is a state of complete nothingness -- total unconsciousness -- or, as tradition tells us, it is a passage of the soul from this world to another.

Consider the first possibility. If death is unconsciousness -- a sleep so deep that not even dreams disturb it -- then death is an extraordinary gain. Think of it this way: if a person were asked to pick out the night when he slept most perfectly, without any dreams at all, and then compare that single night against every other day and night of his life, I believe anyone -- not just an ordinary person, but even the Great King of Persia himself -- would find very few days or nights that were better or more pleasant than that one dreamless sleep.

If death is like that, then I say it is a gain. For all of eternity becomes nothing more than a single perfect night.
elegant:
There are only two possibilities. Either death is total unconsciousness, or it is a passage to another world.

If death is dreamless sleep -- consider how rare and precious one night of perfect, undisturbed rest is. Even the Great King would find few nights to rival it. If death is like that, then all of eternity is just one perfect night. That is no loss. That is a gain.
summary: Socrates presents the first of two possibilities for death: if it is total unconsciousness like dreamless sleep, it is an unspeakable gain, since even the most powerful person rarely experiences a night more pleasant than perfect, undisturbed rest.
distilled: If death is dreamless sleep, then eternity is one perfect night -- and that is a gain, not a loss.
notes: This passage begins the famous 'two possibilities' argument, one of the most influential meditations on death in Western philosophy. The dreamless sleep analogy is striking for its simplicity and accessibility -- it requires no metaphysical commitments, only honest reflection on one's own experience. The reference to 'the great king' (the King of Persia, the wealthiest and most powerful person the Greek world knew) makes the argument universal: if even supreme worldly power cannot purchase many nights better than dreamless sleep, then total unconsciousness is no punishment. The argument is deliberately modest -- Socrates says 'reason to hope,' not certainty. Compare the Phaedo (67d-e), where Socrates develops the more ambitious argument that philosophy is 'practice for death' because it trains the soul to separate from the body. Here the tone is more pragmatic and consolatory. This passage influenced Epicurus' argument that death is 'nothing to us' because where death is, sensation is not. Shakespeare echoes it in Hamlet's 'To sleep, perchance to dream' soliloquy, which inverts Socrates' comfort by worrying about what dreams may come. Cicero and Seneca both cite this passage when discussing the fear of death.

narrative: But if death is the journey to another place, and there, as men say, all the dead abide, what good, O my friends and judges, can be greater than this? If indeed when the pilgrim arrives in the world below, he is delivered from the professors of justice in this world, and finds the true judges who are said to give judgment there, Minos and Rhadamanthus and Aeacus and Triptolemus, and other sons of God who were righteous in their own life, that pilgrimage will be worth making. What would not a man give if he might converse with Orpheus and Musaeus and Hesiod and Homer? Nay, if this be true, let me die again and again. I myself, too, shall have a wonderful interest in there meeting and conversing with Palamedes, and Ajax the son of Telamon, and any other ancient hero who has suffered death through an unjust judgment; and there will be no small pleasure, as I think, in comparing my own sufferings with theirs. Above all, I shall then be able to continue my search into true and false knowledge; as in this world, so also in the next; and I shall find out who is wise, and who pretends to be wise, and is not. What would not a man give, O judges, to be able to examine the leader of the great Trojan expedition; or Odysseus or Sisyphus, or numberless others, men and women too! What infinite delight would there be in conversing with them and asking them questions! In another world they do not put a man to death for asking questions: assuredly not. For besides being happier than we are, they will be immortal, if what is said is true.
refashioned:
But suppose death is a journey to another place, where all the dead gather. What greater good could there be, my friends and judges? If the traveler arrives in the world below and is free of the so-called judges of this world, and instead finds the true judges -- Minos, Rhadamanthus, Aeacus, Triptolemus, and the other divine beings who lived justly -- that journey will be worth making.

What would a person not give to sit and talk with Orpheus, Musaeus, Hesiod, and Homer? If this is true, let me die again and again! I would find extraordinary interest in meeting Palamedes, and Ajax the son of Telamon, and every other hero who suffered death through an unjust verdict. There would be no small pleasure in comparing my own experience with theirs.

But above all -- above all -- I would be able to continue my search for true and false knowledge, in the next world just as in this one. I would discover who is truly wise and who only pretends to be. What would a person not give to examine Agamemnon, the leader of the great expedition to Troy? Or Odysseus? Or Sisyphus? Or countless others, men and women alike? What infinite delight there would be in conversing with them and asking them questions!

And in that other world, they do not execute a man for asking questions. Certainly not. For besides being happier than we are, the dead -- if what is said is true -- are immortal.
elegant:
But if death is a journey to where the dead gather, what could be better? To be free of this world's false judges and meet the true ones. To converse with Orpheus, Homer, Hesiod. To meet Palamedes and Ajax -- heroes who also died by unjust verdicts -- and compare their experience with mine.

Above all, to continue the search for wisdom in the next world as in this one. To examine Agamemnon, Odysseus, and countless others. What infinite delight! And in that world, they do not put a man to death for asking questions.
summary: Socrates presents the second possibility of death: if it is a journey to another world, it offers the supreme goods of meeting the great figures of myth and history, comparing experiences with those unjustly condemned, and -- best of all -- continuing philosophical inquiry forever, in a place where questioning cannot be punished by death.
distilled: If death is a journey to another world, it offers the ultimate gift: the chance to question and learn forever, free from the threat of execution.
notes: This is one of the most celebrated passages in all of Western philosophy and literature. Socrates transforms the terror of death into an adventure of the mind. His catalog of desired conversation partners is carefully chosen: Orpheus and Musaeus represent poetry and mystery; Hesiod and Homer represent the foundations of Greek culture; Palamedes and Ajax represent those unjustly condemned (Palamedes was framed by Odysseus, Ajax was denied Achilles' armor and driven to madness). Socrates implicitly places himself in this lineage of the unjustly judged. The climactic point -- 'above all, I shall then be able to continue my search into true and false knowledge' -- reveals what Socrates values most: not rest, not reunion, but the continuation of philosophical inquiry. The poignant observation that 'in another world they do not put a man to death for asking questions' is both humor and indictment. Compare the Myth of Er in Republic Book X, where Plato develops an elaborate afterlife narrative. The Phaedo (63b-c) echoes this hope when Socrates says he expects to find 'gods who are good masters' and 'men who have died who are better than those here.' The passage influenced Cicero's Tusculan Disputations, Dante's Limbo of the virtuous pagans in Inferno Canto IV, and countless later visions of the philosophical afterlife. Socrates' desire to examine Agamemnon and Odysseus -- to cross-examine the heroes -- is characteristically Socratic: even paradise is imagined as a seminar.

narrative: Wherefore, O judges, be of good cheer about death, and know of a certainty, that no evil can happen to a good man, either in life or after death. He and his are not neglected by the gods; nor has my own approaching end happened by mere chance. But I see clearly that the time had arrived when it was better for me to die and be released from trouble; wherefore the oracle gave no sign. For which reason, also, I am not angry with my condemners, or with my accusers; they have done me no harm, although they did not mean to do me any good; and for this I may gently blame them.
refashioned:
Therefore, judges, be of good cheer about death, and know this with certainty: no evil can happen to a good man, either in life or after death. The gods do not neglect him or those he loves. And my own approaching death has not happened by mere chance. I see clearly that the time had come when it was better for me to die and be released from trouble. That is why the divine sign gave no warning.

For this reason, I am not angry with those who condemned me or those who accused me. They have done me no real harm, even though they certainly did not intend to do me any good. For that much, I may gently blame them.
elegant:
Take heart, judges. No evil can happen to a good man, either in life or after death. The gods do not neglect him. My death is not chance -- the time had come. That is why the sign was silent.

I am not angry with my condemners. They have done me no real harm, though they meant me no good.
summary: Socrates declares the central moral conviction of his life -- that no evil can happen to a good man -- and extends it beyond death. He forgives his accusers, noting they have done him no real harm, though he gently blames them for their intentions.
distilled: No evil can happen to a good man, either in life or after death -- the gods do not neglect him.
notes: The declaration 'no evil can happen to a good man, either in life or after death' is arguably the single most important ethical claim in the Apology, and one of the most consequential sentences in Western moral philosophy. It encapsulates the Socratic (and later Stoic) thesis that virtue is sufficient for happiness -- that external misfortune, including death, cannot constitute genuine harm for the person of good character. This claim is the philosophical foundation of the entire speech: if it is true, then the death sentence is not a punishment but a release. Compare Republic 613a, where Socrates argues that the just man is happy even if the world treats him as unjust. The Stoics adopted this principle as central doctrine -- Epictetus' 'It is not things that disturb us but our judgments about them' is a direct descendant. The gentle forgiveness of his accusers -- 'they have done me no harm, although they did not mean to do me any good' -- combines magnanimity with subtle irony. This echoes the earlier argument (3.2) that unrighteousness harms the doer, not the victim. The Phaedo's closing scenes fulfill the serenity promised here: Socrates drinks the hemlock calmly and comforts his weeping friends. The Christian tradition found in this passage a pagan anticipation of the martyr's faith -- Augustine and Boethius both engage with it directly. The Crito complements this passage by showing that Socrates' acceptance of death is not passive resignation but principled choice: he refuses to escape because doing so would violate the laws he has lived under.

narrative:
Still I have a favour to ask of them. When my sons are grown up, I would ask you, O my friends, to punish them; and I would have you trouble them, as I have troubled you, if they seem to care about riches, or anything, more than about virtue; or if they pretend to be something when they are really nothing,--then reprove them, as I have reproved you, for not caring about that for which they ought to care, and thinking that they are something when they are really nothing. And if you do this, both I and my sons will have received justice at your hands.

"The hour of departure has arrived, and we go our ways--I to die, and you to live. Which is better God only knows."
refashioned:
Still, I have one favor to ask. When my sons are grown, I ask you, my friends, to give them the same trouble I have given you. If they seem to care about wealth, or anything else, more than they care about virtue -- punish them for it. If they pretend to be something when they are really nothing, reprove them as I have reproved you: for not caring about what truly matters, and for imagining themselves to be what they are not.

If you do this, both I and my sons will have received justice at your hands.

The hour of departure has arrived, and we go our separate ways -- I to die, and you to live. Which of these is better, only God knows.
elegant:
I ask one favor. When my sons are grown, trouble them as I have troubled you. If they value wealth over virtue, or pretend to be what they are not, reprove them as I reproved you. Do this, and both I and my sons will have received justice.

The hour of departure has arrived. We go our ways -- I to die, you to live. Which is better, God alone knows.
summary: Socrates makes his final request: that the Athenians hold his sons to the same standard of virtue he held everyone else to, reproving them if they value wealth over goodness. He closes with the most famous lines of the Apology, accepting the unknown with equanimity.
distilled: Socrates' final words accept the ultimate uncertainty with perfect calm: we go our separate ways, and which path is better, only God knows.
notes: The closing lines of the Apology -- 'The hour of departure has arrived, and we go our ways -- I to die, and you to live. Which is better God only knows' -- are among the most famous sentences in the history of philosophy and Western literature. Their power lies in the perfect balance of structure (the chiastic 'I to die, and you to live'), the radical agnosticism ('which is better God only knows'), and the serene acceptance of uncertainty that defines the Socratic temperament. Socrates does not claim death is better than life, nor life better than death -- he simply does not know, and he is at peace with not knowing. This is the epistemological humility of 'I know that I know nothing' applied to the most consequential question a human being can face. The request about his sons is deeply human and connects to the charges against him: he was accused of corrupting the youth, and his final wish is that others do for his own sons what he did for the youth of Athens -- hold them accountable to virtue. Compare Crito 54b-c, where the personified Laws argue that Socrates owes Athens obedience partly because the city raised and educated his children. The Phaedo (116a-b) describes Socrates' final moments with his sons, whom he sends away before drinking the hemlock. The closing line's refusal to claim knowledge of what lies beyond death is consistent with everything Socrates has argued: true wisdom is knowing the limits of one's knowledge. This honest uncertainty proved more influential than any confident assertion could have been -- it became a model for how to face death with courage and intellectual honesty. The Stoics, Montaigne, and the existentialists all found in these words a template for confronting mortality. The Symposium (223d) ends with Socrates still awake and talking at dawn while everyone else has fallen asleep -- a fitting companion image to this final departure, where Socrates walks calmly toward death while the living remain uncertain of their own fortune.
